And just like how Superman has his Fortress of Solitude to help him fight, targeted therapy has special medicine that helps it find and defeat the cancer cells.

In simpler terms, targeted therapy is a way to treat cancer by using medicines that go after the exact cells that are causing the problem, rather than just blanketing the entire body with medicine. It's like using a laser to cut through a specific piece of wood, instead of swinging a big hammer at everything in sight. This means that the medicine is more effective and less likely to have side effects because it's not affecting as many good cells.</s>
easy_definition: A type of treatment that uses drugs or other substances to target specific molecules that cancer cells need to survive and spread. Targeted therapies work in different ways to treat cancer. Some stop cancer cells from growing by interrupting signals that cause them to grow and divide, stopping signals that help form blood vessels, delivering cell-killing substances to cancer cells, or starving cancer cells of hormones they need to grow. Other targeted therapies help the immune system kill cancer cells or directly cause cancer cell death. Most targeted therapies are either small-molecule drugs or monoclonal antibodies. Also called molecularly targeted therapy.

formal_definition: thoracic surgeon is A surgeon who has special training in operating on organs inside the chest, including the heart and lungs.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of "thoracic surgeon" for a non-medical person:

A thoracic surgeon is a type of doctor who specializes in operating on the organs inside your chest, like your heart and lungs. Imagine you have a big box called your body, and inside that box, there are lots of important organs like your heart and lungs. A thoracic surgeon is someone who can open up that box and fix any problems with those organs without making too much mess or hurting you too badly. They are super skilled at doing surgery on these delicate parts, so they can help you feel better and get back to your normal life as soon as possible.</s>
easy_definition: A doctor who’s an expert in operating on organs inside the chest.

formal_definition: ultrasound is A procedure that uses high-energy sound waves to look at tissues and organs inside the body. The sound waves make echoes that form pictures of the tissues and organs on a computer screen (sonogram). Ultrasound may be used to help diagnose diseases, such as cancer. It may also be used during pregnancy to check the fetus (unborn baby) and during medical procedures, such as biopsies. Also called ultrasonography.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain the term "ultrasound" in simple language for a non-medical person:

Ultrasound is like a special tool that doctors use to take pictures of the inside of your body using sound waves. Imagine you're playing a game of sonar with a friend, where you bounce sounds off objects to find them. Well, doctors use something similar to do the same thing, but instead of finding hidden objects, they're looking at your insides! They can see pictures of your organs and tissues on a computer screen, which helps them figure out if there might be any problems or diseases inside you. During pregnancy, ultrasound can even show them how the baby is growing and developing. And during some medical procedures, doctors might use ultrasound to guide them while they operate. So, ultrasound is like a cool tool that helps doctors see what's going on inside your body without having to cut you open!</s>
easy_definition: A test that uses sound waves to take pictures of the inside of the body.

formal_definition: wedge resection is Surgery to remove a triangle-shaped slice of tissue. It may be used to remove a tumor and a small amount of normal tissue around it.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of "wedge resection" that's easy to understand and free of technical jargon:

A wedge resection is a type of surgery where a surgeon removes a small triangle-shaped piece of tissue from your body. Imagine cutting a cake into a wedge shape - that's roughly what happens during a wedge resection. The goal is to remove a tumor or abnormal tissue, along with just a little bit of surrounding healthy tissue. This helps doctors get rid of the problem area while minimizing the impact on the rest of your body.

For example, if you had a tumor growing on your kidney, a surgeon might use a wedge resection to remove just the tumor and some of the surrounding tissue. This would leave most of your kidney intact, which is important because your kidneys play a crucial role in keeping you healthy.

I hope that helps! Let me know if you have any other questions.</s>
easy_definition: An operation that removes a small part of a lobe.

formal_definition: adjuvant therapy is Additional cancer treatment given after the primary treatment to lower the risk that the cancer will come back. Adjuvant therapy may include chemotherapy, radiation therapy, hormone therapy, targeted therapy, or biological therapy.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain the term "adjuvant therapy" in a way that's easy to understand:

Adjuvant therapy is like having a backup plan to keep your cancer from coming back. After the primary treatment (like surgery or chemo), doctors might give you extra treatments to help lower the chance of the cancer growing again. These could be different medicines like chemotherapy, hormone therapy, or targeted therapy. They work together with the primary treatment to give your body the best chance of staying healthy. It's like having a superhero team working together to save the day!</s>
easy_definition: Additional cancer treatment given after the primary treatment to lower the risk that the cancer will come back. Adjuvant therapy may include chemotherapy, radiation therapy, hormone therapy, targeted therapy, or biological therapy.

formal_definition: adrenal gland is One of a pair of small, triangle-shaped glands on top of each kidney. Each adrenal gland has two parts: the adrenal cortex (the outer layer of the adrenal gland) and the adrenal medulla (the center of the adrenal gland). The adrenal glands make steroid hormones, adrenaline, and noradrenaline. These hormones help control many important body functions, including heart rate, blood pressure, blood sugar, metabolism, and the way the body reacts to stress. They also balance water and salt in the body and make hormones involved in the development of sex characteristics. Also called suprarenal gland.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to explain the term "adrenal gland" in a simple and easy-to-understand way!

So, you know how your body has different systems that help it work properly, like your digestive system or your circulatory system? Well, the adrenal glands are part of another important system called the endocrine system. They're like little superheroes inside your body that help keep everything running smoothly. They produce special chemicals called hormones that help regulate things like your heart rate, blood pressure, and how your body responds to stress. They even help you feel alert and awake during the day! It's like they give your body a boost of energy when you need it most. And just like how you have one of these superheroes on each side of your body, you have two adrenal glands, one on top of each kidney. So, next time you hear someone talking about their adrenal glands, you'll know exactly what they're talking about!</s>
easy_definition: A small organ on top of each kidney that makes hormones.

formal_definition: anus is The opening of the rectum to the outside of the body.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining the term "anus" in simple language for a non-medical person:

The anus is like a little doorway at the bottom of your tushy that leads to the outside world. It's where your body gets rid of waste, like poop, by pushing it out through this opening. Just like how you might open a door to go inside or outside a room, your body opens the anus to get rid of things it doesn't need anymore. It's a very important part of keeping your body healthy!</s>
easy_definition: The opening of the intestinal tract between the legs through which stool passes out of the body.

formal_definition: appendix is A small, fingerlike pouch that sticks out from the cecum (the first part of the large intestine near the end of the small intestine).
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining the term "appendix" in a way that's easy to understand for a non-medical person:

So, did you know that your body has a little extra pocket inside your tummy? It's called your appendix! It's like a tiny little flag that sticks out from the main part of your intestines. It's not super important most of the time, but sometimes it can get inflamed or infected and cause problems. That's why doctors might need to take it out if it gets sick. Just like how you might have a spare tire in your car in case the one on your car breaks down, your body has an appendix as a backup plan for when your main intestines get sick.</s>
easy_definition: A small tube-like organ attached to the first section of the large intestine.

formal_definition: carcinoid syndrome is A combination of symptoms caused by the release of serotonin and other substances from carcinoid tumors of the gastrointestinal tract. Symptoms may include flushing of the face, flat angiomas (small collections of dilated blood vessels) of the skin, diarrhea, bronchial spasms, rapid pulse, and sudden drops in blood pressure.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt at explaining carcinoid syndrome in simple language:

Carcinoid syndrome is like a secret ingredient in your favorite recipe. Imagine you have a tummy tumor that's releasing special chemicals into your body. These chemicals can cause some pretty weird things to happen, like your face getting hot and red, little red bumps on your skin, your heart beating fast, and your blood pressure dropping suddenly. It's kind of like a mystery illness that doctors need to figure out how to solve!</s>
easy_definition: A group of symptoms that happen when carcinoid tumors release serotonin and other chemicals into the blood.

formal_definition: carcinoid tumor is A slow-growing type of tumor usually found in the gastrointestinal system (most often in the small intestine and rectum), and sometimes in the lungs or other sites. Carcinoid tumors may spread to the liver or other sites in the body, and they may secrete substances such as serotonin or prostaglandins, causing carcinoid syndrome.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain "carcinoid tumor" in simple language:

A carcinoid tumor is like a mischievous little monster that grows in your body. It usually starts in the digestive system, like the small intestine or rectum, and sometimes moves to other parts of the body like the lungs or liver. This bad boy can cause problems by secreting special chemicals into your bloodstream, which can lead to some pretty weird symptoms like flushing, diarrhea, and even trouble breathing. It's important to catch these tumors early so we can treat them before they get too big and cause more trouble. Think of it like a tiny monster that needs to be tamed!</s>
easy_definition: A neuroendocrine tumor found most commonly in the gastrointestinal tract, lungs, bronchi, thymus, and other areas in the body. It may secrete the hormone serotonin and other chemicals.

formal_definition: colon is The longest part of the large intestine (a tube-like organ connected to the small intestine at one end and the anus at the other). The colon removes water and some nutrients and electrolytes from partially digested food. The remaining material, solid waste called stool, moves through the colon, is stored in the rectum, and leaves the body through the anus. The colon is part of the digestive system.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt at explaining the term "colon" in simple language:

The colon is like a long, hollow tube that connects your small intestine to your anus. It helps remove extra water and some nutrients from the food you eat before it leaves your body as poop. Think of it like a filter for your digestive system! The colon stores the leftover food waste in its storage area, called the rectum, until it's ready to come out through the anus. So, the colon plays a big role in getting rid of waste and keeping your digestive system running smoothly.</s>
easy_definition: The longest part of the large intestine (a tube-like organ connected to the small intestine at one end and the anus at the other). The colon removes water and some nutrients and electrolytes from partially digested food. The remaining material, solid waste called stool, moves through the colon, is stored in the rectum, and leaves the body through the anus. The colon is part of the digestive system.

formal_definition: cortisol is A hormone made by the adrenal cortex (the outer layer of the adrenal gland). It helps the body use glucose (a sugar), protein, and fats. Cortisol made in the laboratory is called hydrocortisone. It is used to treat many conditions, including inflammation, allergies, and some cancers. Cortisol is a type of glucocorticoid hormone.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain the term "cortisol" in simple language:

Cortisol is a special kind of hormone that helps your body use energy from food. Imagine your body is like a fireplace, and cortisol is the match that helps light the flames. When you eat food, your body uses cortisol to turn that food into usable energy for your muscles, brain, and other parts of your body. This helps you feel alert and energized, and it also helps your body fight off infections and injuries. Some people take cortisol medication when their own bodies don't make enough of it, or when they need extra help using energy from food.

I hope this explanation helps! Let me know if you have any questions.</s>
easy_definition: A hormone that controls blood sugar, metabolism, and other functions in the body.

formal_definition: Cushing syndrome is A condition in which there is too much cortisol (a hormone made by the outer layer of the adrenal gland) in the body. Cushing syndrome may be caused by taking too many steroid drugs or by certain types of tumors. Tumors that make adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) cause the adrenal gland to make too much cortisol. Symptoms of Cushing syndrome include a round face, thin arms and legs, severe fatigue and muscle weakness, high blood pressure, high blood sugar, purple or pink stretch marks on the skin, and weight gain, especially in the abdomen. Also called hypercortisolism.
simplified_explanation: Term 'Cushing syndrome' not found in medical dictionary.
easy_definition: A condition caused by the release of excess cortisol in the body.

formal_definition: duodenum is The first part of the small intestine. It connects to the stomach. The duodenum helps to further digest food coming from the stomach. It absorbs nutrients (vitamins, minerals, carbohydrates, fats, proteins) and water from food so they can be used by the body.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the term "duodenum" that's easy to understand and relatable:

The duodenum is like a long, narrow tube that connects your stomach to your small intestine. Imagine your stomach as a big bucket that can hold lots of food, like a big meal. When you eat, your stomach breaks down the food into smaller pieces using special juices and enzymes. Then, the duodenum takes over and helps your body absorb all the good stuff - like vitamins, minerals, carbohydrates, fats, and proteins - so you can use them for energy or grow strong. Think of it like a sponge, soaking up all the important things your body needs. Pretty cool, right?</s>
easy_definition: A part of the digestive tract that receives food from the stomach and mixes it with digestive juices.

formal_definition: endoscope is A thin, tube-like instrument used to look at tissues inside the body. An endoscope has a light and a lens for viewing and may have a tool to remove tissue.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the term "endoscope" that's easy to understand and relatable to everyday life:

An endoscope is like a long, thin telescope that doctors use to look inside your body. It has a special light and lens that helps them see things they couldn't see otherwise. Sometimes, the endoscope also has tools on the end that can help them remove something from your body, like a tiny pair of scissors or a small vacuum cleaner. Just like how you might use a telescope to look at the stars or a magnifying glass to check out a bug up close, an endoscope lets doctors take a closer look at what's going on inside you.</s>
easy_definition: A device that is passed through a natural opening to do work inside the body.

formal_definition: enucleation is In medicine, the removal of an organ or tumor in such a way that it comes out clean and whole, like a nut from its shell.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of "enucleation" in simple language:

Enucleation means removing something, like a tumor or an organ, in a special way so that it comes out complete and intact, like a nut coming out of its shell. It's like peeling off the shell to reveal the delicious nut inside. In medicine, doctors use this technique to remove things that are causing problems without damaging the surrounding tissue. For example, they might use enucleation to remove a cancerous tumor from a kidney without affecting the rest of the kidney.</s>
easy_definition: Removal of an organ or tumor in such a way that it comes out clean and whole, like a nut from its shell.

formal_definition: gastrin is A hormone released from special cells in the lining of the stomach after eating. Gastrin causes the stomach to release an acid that helps digest food.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt at explaining the term "gastrin" in simple language:

Gastrin is a special kind of hormone that comes from cells in your tummy. When you eat food, these cells send a message to your stomach to start making acid to help break down what you're eating. It's like when you mix baking soda and vinegar in a volcano - the acid makes a fizzing sound and helps turn your food into nutrients your body can use. Just like how your stomach needs acid to digest food, gastrin helps your stomach do its job!</s>
easy_definition: A hormone made and released by the stomach.

formal_definition: genetic testing is A laboratory method that looks for changes in genes, gene expression, or chromosomes in a person’s cells or tissues. Genetic testing may be done to look for inherited changes in a person's genes that may be a sign that the person has an increased risk of developing a specific disease or condition or of having a child or other family member with the disease or condition. This type of genetic testing is called germline testing. Genetic testing is also done on tumor tissue to look for genetic changes in the cancer cells. These changes occur during a person’s lifetime and are not inherited from a parent. This type of genetic testing, called somatic testing, is done to learn more about the cancer, plan treatment, or find out how well treatment is working.
simplified_explanation:
Genetic testing is like a detective story where we use special tools to look for clues in your body. We check if there are any changes in your DNA, which are like the instructions for making you. These changes could mean you might get sick or have a baby with a sickness later on. We do this test to help predict what might happen and figure out the best way to stop it from happening. It's like solving a mystery!

Sometimes, these changes happen when you're growing inside your mom's tummy, and sometimes they happen after you're born. The test looks at the cells in your body, like little soldiers following orders, and checks if they're doing their job right. If some of them are acting weird, we call that a "mutation." It's like a mistake in the blueprints. We can then figure out what to do next to fix the problem.

There are two kinds of genetic tests: one looks for changes that might affect you, and the other looks at changes in the bad guys, the cancer cells. For both kinds, we try to find answers so we can help keep you healthy!</s>
easy_definition: A laboratory method that looks for changes in genes, gene expression, or chromosomes in a person’s cells or tissues. Genetic testing may be done to look for inherited changes in a person's genes that may be a sign that the person has an increased risk of developing a specific disease or condition or of having a child or other family member with the disease or condition. This type of genetic testing is called germline testing. Genetic testing is also done on tumor tissue to look for genetic changes in the cancer cells. These changes occur during a person’s lifetime and are not inherited from a parent. This type of genetic testing, called somatic testing, is done to learn more about the cancer, plan treatment, or find out how well treatment is working.

formal_definition: glucagon is A hormone produced by the pancreas that increases the level of glucose (sugar) in the blood.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my attempt at explaining the term "glucagon" in simple language:

Glucagon is like a magic pill that helps keep your blood sugar levels just right. When your blood sugar gets too low, your body releases glucagon into your bloodstream. This hormone tells your liver to release stored sugar into your blood, which helps bring your blood sugar back up to a healthy level. It's kind of like how your body has a built-in backup plan to keep you energized and focused throughout the day!</s>
easy_definition: A hormone made by the pancreas that works with insulin to control blood sugar levels.

formal_definition: hereditary is In medicine, describes the passing of genetic information from parent to child through the genes in sperm and egg cells. Also called inherited.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain the term "hereditary" in a way that's easy to understand for a non-medical person:

Hereditary means something that is passed down from parents to their children through their genes. It's like a special set of instructions that are inside every cell in your body, telling them how to grow and work properly. These instructions are passed on from generation to generation through the sperm and egg cells that make up each family. So, if your mom or dad has a certain trait or condition, there's a good chance you might inherit it too. It's like a family recipe book - you get the same ingredients (genes) from your parents, and they help shape who you are and how your body works.</s>
easy_definition: Passed down from parent to child through coded information in cells.

formal_definition: hives is Itchy, raised red areas on the skin. Hives are caused by a reaction to certain foods, drugs, infections, or emotional stress. Also called urticaria.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "hives" to a non-medical person:

Hives are like little bumps on your skin that can be red, itchy, and uncomfortable. They can appear anywhere on your body, like on your arms, legs, or torso. They're caused by something inside your body reacting to something outside of it, like a certain food or medicine, or even stress. It's kind of like when you have an allergic reaction to something, but instead of sneezing or congestion, you get these little bumps on your skin. And just like how you need to take medicine to feel better when you're sick, there are things you can do to make hives go away too. So don't worry, hives aren't anything to be scared of, they're just your body's way of telling you something's not right and needs some attention.</s>
easy_definition: A skin rash caused by the body trying to rid itself of a foreign substance.

formal_definition: hypothalamus is The area of the brain that controls body temperature, hunger, and thirst.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "hypothalamus" to a non-medical person:

Imagine your brain as a super cooler for your body. It's like a thermostat that keeps your body at just the right temperature. That's what the hypothalamus does - it helps regulate your body's temperature, hunger, and thirst. So, if you're feeling hot or cold, the hypothalamus adjusts your body's temperature to keep you comfortable. And when you're hungry or thirsty, it sends signals to your body to eat or drink something. It's like a tiny boss that keeps everything running smoothly inside your head!</s>
easy_definition: A part of the brain that works with the nervous system and glands that make hormones in the body.

formal_definition: ileum is The last part of the small intestine. It connects to the cecum (first part of the large intestine). The ileum helps to further digest food coming from the stomach and other parts of the small intestine. It absorbs nutrients (vitamins, minerals, carbohydrates, fats, proteins) and water from food so they can be used by the body.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "ileum" to a non-medical person:

The ileum is like a special part of your digestive system that helps break down the food you eat into tiny little pieces that your body can use for energy. It's like a big, long tube that connects your stomach to your large intestine, and it has lots of tiny little hairs inside that help tear apart the food and absorb all the good stuff, like vitamins and minerals. So, when you eat something, it goes through your mouth, into your stomach, and then into your ileum, where it gets broken down even more before moving on to your large intestine and finally out of your body as poop. Makes sense?</s>
easy_definition: The last section of the small intestine.

formal_definition: imaging is In medicine, a process that makes pictures of areas inside the body. Imaging uses methods such as x-rays (high-energy radiation), ultrasound (high-energy sound waves), and radio waves.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain the term "imaging" in a way that's easy to understand for a non-medical person:

Imaging is like taking a picture of the inside of your body using special tools. Just like how you take pictures of the outside of your body with a camera, imaging helps doctors see what's going on inside. There are different types of imaging, like X-rays, which use high-energy radiation to create pictures, and ultrasound, which uses high-energy sound waves. These images can help doctors figure out if there's something wrong and where it is in your body. It's like having a built-in superpower to see inside your own body without having to cut open your skin!</s>
easy_definition: A test that makes pictures (images) of the insides of the body.

formal_definition: immune system is A complex network of cells, tissues, organs, and the substances they make that helps the body fight infections and other diseases. The immune system includes white blood cells and organs and tissues of the lymph system, such as the thymus, spleen, tonsils, lymph nodes, lymph vessels, and bone marrow.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining the concept of the immune system in simple language, using everyday analogies and avoiding technical jargon:

The immune system is like a superhero team that protects your body from bad guys like germs and viruses. It's made up of special cells and organs that work together to keep you healthy. Just like how you have police officers who patrol the streets to catch criminals, your immune system has different types of cells that roam around your body looking for any danger. When they find something harmful, they fight it off with their own special powers. For example, some cells can recognize and destroy infected cells, while others can release chemicals to make the germs weak and unable to cause trouble. Your immune system is like a well-trained army, always on the lookout for threats and ready to defend you at a moment's notice.</s>
easy_definition: The body’s natural defense against infection and disease.

formal_definition: insulin is A hormone made by the islet cells of the pancreas. Insulin controls the amount of sugar in the blood by moving it into the cells, where it can be used by the body for energy.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining the term "insulin" in simple language that a non-medical person could understand:

Insulin is like a key that unlocks the door to your body's cells, allowing sugar to enter and be used as fuel. Without insulin, sugar would build up in your bloodstream and cause problems. The pancreas, a small organ near your stomach, makes insulin and releases it into your blood when needed. It's like a tiny factory inside you that keeps your body running smoothly. When you eat food, especially sugary foods, your body needs insulin to help process it and use the sugar for energy. Without enough insulin, your body can't use sugar properly, which can lead to problems like diabetes.</s>
easy_definition: A chemical that controls the amount of sugar in the blood. It lowers the blood sugar levels.

formal_definition: jejunum is The middle part of the small intestine. It is between the duodenum (first part of the small intestine) and the ileum (last part of the small intestine). The jejunum helps to further digest food coming from the stomach. It absorbs nutrients (vitamins, minerals, carbohydrates, fats, proteins) and water from food so they can be used by the body.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of the term "jejunum" in simple language:

The jejunum is like a long, thin tube inside your belly that helps break down the food you eat. It's like a big pipe that takes the food from your stomach and breaks it down into tiny pieces so your body can use the nutrients and energy it needs. It's like a magic machine that turns food into fuel for your body! And just like how you need different machines for different tasks, your body has different parts of the small intestine for different things, and the jejunum is one of them. It's very important for keeping you healthy and strong!</s>
easy_definition: The middle section of the small intestine.

formal_definition: liver is A large organ located in the upper abdomen. The liver cleanses the blood and aids in digestion by secreting bile.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "liver" to a non-medical person:

Hey there! So, you know how your body has different organs that help it work properly? Well, the liver is one of the most important ones. It's like a superhero organ that helps keep your body clean and healthy. It's located in your tummy, just below your ribcage, and it does lots of cool things like cleaning your blood and helping with digestion. It's like a magic filter that makes sure your blood stays pure and clean, so your body can function properly. Pretty cool, right?</s>
easy_definition: The largest organ and gland in the body with many vital functions.

formal_definition: lung is One of a pair of organs in the chest that supplies the body with oxygen, and removes carbon dioxide from the body.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "lung" to a non-medical person:

Hey there! So, you know how you breathe in air and it goes into your body? Well, inside your body, there are two special organs called lungs. They're like two big balloons in your chest that take in oxygen from the air you breathe and give your body what it needs to stay alive. At the same time, they get rid of something called carbon dioxide, which is like a bad guy that your body doesn't need anymore. It's kind of like how your liver takes out the trash for your body - the lungs do the same thing for your breathing! Without these lungs, your body wouldn't be able to get the oxygen it needs, and you would feel really sick and tired all the time.</s>
easy_definition: One of a pair of organs that consists of airways and air sacs.

formal_definition: observation is In medicine, watching a patient’s condition but not giving treatment unless symptoms appear or change.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "observation" in a way that's easy to understand for a non-medical person:

Observation means watching someone's health without giving any special treatment or medication unless their condition changes or they show new symptoms. It's like when you observe a bird building its nest - you don't touch it or try to fix it, you just watch from a distance to see how it progresses. In medicine, we do the same with patients - we keep an eye on them to see if their condition improves or gets worse, but we don't give any treatments unless they need them.</s>
easy_definition: In medicine, watching a patient’s condition but not giving treatment unless symptoms appear or change.

formal_definition: palliative care is Care given to improve the quality of life and help reduce pain in people who have a serious or life-threatening disease, such as cancer. The goal of palliative care is to prevent or treat, as early as possible, the symptoms of the disease and the side effects caused by treatment of the disease. It also attends to the psychological, social, and spiritual problems caused by the disease or its treatment. For cancer, palliative care may include therapies, such as surgery, radiation therapy, or chemotherapy, to remove, shrink, or slow the growth of a tumor that is causing pain. It may also include family and caregiver support. Palliative care may be given with other treatments from the time of diagnosis until the end of life.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the term "palliative care" in simple language:

Palliative care is like a gentle hand on your shoulder, helping to ease the pain and discomfort of a serious illness. It's not about curing the illness, but rather about making you feel better and improving your quality of life. The team provides specialized care to manage the symptoms of the illness and its side effects, like pain, nausea, or breathing trouble. They also focus on your emotional well-being, supporting you and your loved ones through this difficult time. Palliative care can be given along with other treatments, like medication or surgery, to help you feel more comfortable and relaxed. It's there to help you live as well as possible, even if your illness isn't going away.</s>
easy_definition: Care given to improve the quality of life and help reduce pain in people who have a serious or life-threatening disease, such as cancer. The goal of palliative care is to prevent or treat, as early as possible, the symptoms of the disease and the side effects caused by treatment of the disease. It also attends to the psychological, social, and spiritual problems caused by the disease or its treatment. For cancer, palliative care may include therapies, such as surgery, radiation therapy, or chemotherapy, to remove, shrink, or slow the growth of a tumor that is causing pain. It may also include family and caregiver support. Palliative care may be given with other treatments from the time of diagnosis until the end of life.

formal_definition: pancreas is A glandular organ located in the abdomen. It makes pancreatic juices, which contain enzymes that aid in digestion, and it produces several hormones, including insulin. The pancreas is surrounded by the stomach, intestines, and other organs.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain the term "pancreas" in simple language:

Hey there! So, you know how your body needs food to have energy and stay healthy? Well, the pancreas is like a little helper that helps make sure everything runs smoothly. It's a gland near your stomach that makes special juices that help break down food into tiny pieces so your body can use them for energy. Plus, it also makes some super important hormones that help control how much sugar is in your bloodstream. Without the pancreas, your body wouldn't be able to do these things properly, and you might get sick or feel really tired all the time. So, let's give a big cheer for the pancreas! 🎉</s>
easy_definition: An organ that makes fluids that help digest food and hormones that control blood sugar.

formal_definition: pelvis is The bones between the lower abdomen and upper thighs that connect the spine to the legs. The pelvis supports upper body weight, aids balance and walking, and protects the bladder, reproductive organs, colon, and rectum. The pelvis can also describe the pelvic region, or the area of the body below the abdomen that contains the hip bones, bladder, and rectum. In females, it also contains the vagina, cervix, uterus, fallopian tubes, and ovaries. In males, it also contains the prostate and seminal vesicles.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's how I would explain the term "pelvis" to a non-medical person:

So, have you ever sat on a chair or a couch and felt the curve of your bottom? That curved shape is kind of like the pelvis! The pelvis is a group of bones down there between your hips and knees that helps keep your body stable and balanced. It also protects some important organs like your bladder, reproductive system, and digestive system. In girls, the pelvis also holds their uterus and other lady parts. And in guys, it holds their prostate gland and other stuff too! Pretty cool, right?</s>
easy_definition: The body area between the hipbones.

formal_definition: pineal gland is A tiny organ in the cerebrum that produces melatonin. Also called pineal body and pineal organ.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "pineal gland" to a non-medical person:

The pineal gland is a small organ inside your brain that helps control your sleep and wakefulness. It's kind of like a tiny alarm clock that tells your body when it's time to sleep or be awake. It makes a hormone called melatonin that helps regulate these things. Imagine having a little voice in your head that tells you when it's time to go to bed or get up in the morning - that's what the pineal gland does!</s>
easy_definition: A small gland in the cerebrum of the brain.

formal_definition: pituitary gland is A pea-sized organ attached to the part of the brain called the hypothalamus. It lies at the base of the brain above the back of the nose. The hypothalamus sends signals to the pituitary gland, which then makes hormones that control other glands and many of the body’s functions, including growth and fertility.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's an explanation of the pituitary gland for a non-medical person:

Imagine your brain is like a big computer that controls all the different parts of your body. Inside that brain, there's a small office called the hypothalamus that sends messages to another part of the brain called the pituitary gland. This little gland is like a factory that makes special chemicals that help all the other glands in your body work properly. These chemicals control things like how tall you grow, when you start growing hair, and even whether you can have kids. So, the pituitary gland is kind of like the boss of all the other glands in your body!</s>
easy_definition: An organ in the brain that controls certain body functions and other hormone glands. Also called the 'master gland'.

formal_definition: primary treatment is The first treatment given for a disease. It is often part of a standard set of treatments, such as surgery followed by chemotherapy and radiation. When used by itself, primary treatment is the one accepted as the best treatment. If it doesn’t cure the disease or it causes severe side effects, other treatment may be added or used instead. Also called first-line therapy, induction therapy, and primary therapy.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the term "primary treatment" that's easy to understand and free from technical jargon:

Think of primary treatment like the foundation of a building. Just like how a strong foundation is needed to support a sturdy house, primary treatment is the first step in treating a disease. It's usually the most effective way to get rid of the problem, but sometimes it might not work on its own. In that case, doctors might add more treatments on top of the primary treatment to finish the job. So, primary treatment is like the base layer of your medical care plan.</s>
easy_definition: The main treatment used to rid the body of cancer.

formal_definition: progression is In medicine, the course of a disease, such as cancer, as it becomes worse or spreads in the body.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the term "progression" in simple language that a non-medical person can understand:

Progression means how something gets worse or spreads over time. In medicine, we use this term to talk about how a disease like cancer is getting worse inside your body. It's like a snowball rolling down a hill - it starts small, but keeps growing bigger and faster until it's too big to stop. Just like how a snowball grows bigger as it rolls down a hill, a disease can grow worse as it spreads through your body. The more it spreads, the harder it is to stop or reverse its progress. So, understanding how a disease is progressing is important for doctors to figure out the best way to treat it.</s>
easy_definition: In medicine, the course of a disease, such as cancer, as it becomes worse or spreads in the body.

formal_definition: prostate is A gland in the male reproductive system. The prostate surrounds the part of the urethra (the tube that empties the bladder) just below the bladder, and produces a fluid that forms part of the semen.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's an explanation of the term "prostate" that meets your requirements:

The prostate is a small gland located near the bottom of the bladder in men. It plays a crucial role in making sure semen is healthy and strong during reproduction. Imagine the prostate as a tiny factory inside the body that helps create the special liquid that carries sperm out of the body during ejaculation. Just like how a factory needs workers to produce goods, the prostate produces a special fluid that helps sperm travel through the semen. Without this gland, fertility and reproduction wouldn't be possible. So, the next time you hear someone talk about their prostate, you'll know exactly what they're talking about!</s>
easy_definition: A male gland that makes fluid that protects sperm from the acid in the vagina.

formal_definition: radiopharmaceutical is A drug that contains a radioactive substance and is used to diagnose or treat disease, including cancer. Also called radioactive drug.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining the term "radiopharmaceutical" in simple language:

A radiopharmaceutical is a special kind of medicine that has a tiny amount of a radioactive substance inside it. When you take this medicine, the radioactivity helps doctors see what's going on inside your body or treat a sickness like cancer. It's like a superhero for your body, helping doctors figure out what's wrong and fix it! Just like how you might use a flashlight to find something in the dark, the radioactivity in a radiopharmaceutical helps doctors shine a light on what's happening inside your body.</s>
easy_definition: A drug that carries a certain amount of radioactive material.

formal_definition: rectum is The last several inches of the large intestine closest to the anus.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of the term "rectum":

Hey there! So, you know how food goes through your digestive system and comes out as poop? Well, the rectum is like the last stop before that poop leaves your body. It's like a little storage room for the food waste that's still getting digested. The rectum is located near the anus, which is the hole where the poop comes out. When the muscles in the rectum relax, the poop moves into the anus and comes out of your body. It's kind of like when you're storing up your homework until you have time to finish it – the rectum is like a place for your body to store up all the leftover food until it's ready to be flushed away!</s>
easy_definition: An organ that holds stool until expelled from the body.

formal_definition: serotonin is A substance that is found mostly in the digestive tract, central nervous system (brain and spinal cord), and platelets. Serotonin acts both as a neurotransmitter (a substance that nerves use to send messages to one another) and a vasoconstrictor (a substance that causes blood vessels to narrow). A low level of serotonin in the brain is thought to be a cause of depression. Also called 5-hydroxytryptamine.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain the term "serotonin" in simple language:

Serotonin is like a special messenger in your body. It helps nerve cells talk to each other and can also help your blood vessels narrow or open up. Some people might have too little serotonin in their brains, which could lead to feelings of sadness or depression. Imagine if you were trying to send a message to a friend through a busy mailroom - serotonin helps make sure the message gets delivered correctly!</s>
easy_definition: A hormone that sends signals between nerve cells and controls things like mood, sleep, and memory.

formal_definition: stomach is An organ that is part of the digestive system. The stomach helps digest food by mixing it with digestive juices and churning it into a thin liquid.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the term "stomach" for a non-medical person:

The stomach is like a big mixing bowl inside your tummy. It helps break down the food we eat into tiny pieces so our body can use it for energy or absorb the nutrients. Imagine you're making a smoothie in a blender - the stomach does something similar, but way more important because it helps our body stay healthy. The stomach uses special juices to mix the food around and churn it up until it's nice and smooth. Then, the nutrients can pass through a tiny hole called the esophagus and go to other parts of the body where they're needed.</s>
easy_definition: An organ of the digestive system that turns solid food into a more liquid form.

formal_definition: testicle is One of two egg-shaped glands inside the scrotum that produce sperm and male hormones. Also called testis.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my attempt at explaining the term "testicle" in simple language for a non-medical person:

Hey there! So, you know how we have a mommy tummy where babies grow, right? Well, guys have something similar called testicles. They're like two little balls in your dad's pants that help make baby boys. Inside those balls, there are special cells called sperm that help create the baby. These cells also help make some important hormones that help keep you feeling manly. It's kind of like how your mom's tummy makes all the stuff you need to grow strong and healthy!</s>
easy_definition: One of a pair of egg-shaped glands found inside the sac between the legs of a man.

formal_definition: thymus is An organ that is part of the lymphatic system, in which T lymphocytes grow and multiply. The thymus is in the chest behind the breastbone.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my attempt at explaining the term "thymus" in a way that's easy to understand for a non-medical person:

The thymus is like a special factory inside your body where immune cells called T cells are made. These T cells are important because they help keep you healthy by fighting off bad guys like viruses and bacteria. The thymus is located in your chest, behind your breastbone, and it's where these T cells grow and multiply before they go out into your body to do their job. It's kind of like how a factory produces cars, but instead of cars, the thymus makes T cells that help protect you from getting sick.</s>
easy_definition: A gland that is behind the breastbone.

formal_definition: tumor marker is A substance found in tissue, blood, bone marrow, or other body fluids that may be a sign of cancer or certain benign (noncancer) conditions. Many tumor markers are proteins made by both normal cells and cancer cells, but they are made in higher amounts by cancer cells. Genetic changes in tumor tissue, such as gene mutations, patterns of gene expression, and other changes in tumor DNA or RNA, are also being used as tumor markers. A tumor marker may be used with other tests to help diagnose cancer. It may also be used to help plan treatment, give a likely prognosis, and find out how well treatment is working or if cancer has come back. Examples of tumor markers include CA-125 (in ovarian cancer), estrogen receptor and progesterone receptor (in breast cancer), CEA (in colon cancer), PCA3 mRNA and PSA (in prostate cancer), and EGFR gene mutation (in non-small cell lung cancer).
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain "tumor marker" in simple language for a non-medical person:

A tumor marker is like a special light that shines on a specific area of your body. This light shows doctors if there are any changes happening inside you that might be related to cancer. The light looks for tiny clues, called biomarkers, that are only present when cancer cells are growing. These clues can be found in things like your blood, bone marrow, or even in your urine! Doctors use these clues to figure out if you have cancer, and if so, what kind of cancer it might be. They can also use them to see how well your treatment is working or if the cancer comes back. Think of it like a special tool that helps doctors solve a mystery - the clues from the tumor marker light help them figure out what's going on inside your body!</s>
easy_definition: A substance found in body tissue or fluid that may be a sign of cancer.

formal_definition: ulcer is A break on the skin, in the lining of an organ, or on the surface of a tissue. An ulcer forms when the surface cells become inflamed, die, and are shed. Ulcers may be linked to cancer and other diseases.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain the term "ulcer" in simple language for a non-medical person:

An ulcer is like a small wound or tear in your body. It happens when the top layer of skin or tissue gets irritated or damaged, causing the cells to die and flake off. This can happen anywhere in your body, like on your stomach, intestines, or even on your skin. Sometimes, ulcers can be a sign of a bigger problem like cancer or another disease. So, if you notice any unusual pain or discomfort, it's important to see a doctor to get checked out.</s>
easy_definition: A sore on the skin or mucous membrane in the body.

formal_definition: x-ray is A type of radiation used in the diagnosis and treatment of cancer and other diseases. In low doses, x-rays are used to diagnose diseases by making pictures of the inside of the body. In high doses, x-rays are used to treat cancer.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the term "x-ray" that's easy to understand for a non-medical person:

An x-ray is like a special kind of light that can see inside our bodies. Just like how we use flashlights to see things in the dark, x-rays can see inside our bodies and take pictures of what's going on inside. But instead of seeing things like toys or a dark room, x-rays can see things like broken bones or tumors that might be hidden from view. X-rays can even help doctors find out if there are any problems with our insides before they become serious. And if needed, x-rays can also be used to treat some types of cancer by giving us a big dose of the same kind of light that can kill bad cells.

I hope that helps! Let me know if you have any other questions.</s>
easy_definition: A test that uses small amounts of radiation to make pictures of the insides of the body. Also called a plain radiograph.

formal_definition: biomarker is A biological molecule found in blood, other body fluids, or tissues that is a sign of a normal or abnormal process, or of a condition or disease. A biomarker may be used to see how well the body responds to a treatment for a disease or condition. Also called molecular marker and signature molecule.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the term "biomarker" that's easy to understand:

A biomarker is like a special kind of fingerprint for your body. It's a tiny piece of information inside your cells or blood that tells doctors how your body is doing. Just like how a police officer might use a fingerprint to identify a criminal, doctors can use biomarkers to figure out if something is going wrong inside your body. For example, if you have high levels of a certain biomarker in your blood, it could mean you have a disease like cancer. Or, if you have low levels of a different biomarker, it could mean you're not getting enough of a certain nutrient. Biomarkers can even show how well a treatment is working! So, they're like little messengers inside your body that tell doctors what's going on.</s>
easy_definition: A biological molecule found in blood, other body fluids, or tissues that is a sign of a normal or abnormal process, or of a condition or disease. A biomarker may be used to see how well the body responds to a treatment for a disease or condition. Also called molecular marker and signature molecule.

formal_definition: core needle biopsy is The removal of a tissue sample with a wide needle for examination under a microscope. Also called core biopsy.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain "core needle biopsy" in simple language:

A core needle biopsy is like taking a small sample of cake batter from a larger piece of cake. Just like how we take a tiny bit of cake to see what it's made of, doctors use a special needle to take a tiny piece of your body to look at under a microscope. This helps them figure out if there might be something wrong with your body and how to fix it. It's a quick and easy way to get important information without having to cut open your whole body.</s>
easy_definition: The removal of a tissue sample with a wide needle for examination under a microscope. Also called core biopsy.

formal_definition: DNA is The molecule inside cells that contains the genetic information needed for a person and most other organisms to develop and grow and is passed from one generation to the next. DNA is made up of two strands that twist into the shape of a spiral ladder called a double helix. Each strand has a backbone that is made up of sugar and phosphate molecules that attach to one of four bases: adenine (A), thymine (T), guanine (G), and cytosine (C). The bases pair up with one another (A with T, and G with C) to form chemical bonds, which act like rungs on a ladder. This holds the two strands of DNA together. Also called deoxyribonucleic acid.
simplified_explanation: Term 'DNA' not found in medical dictionary.
easy_definition: Deoxyribonucleic acid.

formal_definition: maintenance therapy is Treatment that is given to help keep cancer from coming back after it has disappeared following the initial therapy. It may include treatment with drugs, vaccines, or antibodies that kill cancer cells, and it may be given for a long time.
simplified_explanation:
Okay, here's my attempt:

Maintenance therapy is like a home security system for your body. After you've gotten rid of the bad guys (cancer cells), you need to keep them from coming back. So, you install a special kind of protection (maintenance therapy) to stop them from sneaking back in. This might involve taking medicine, getting vaccines, or using other tools to keep your body safe. Just like how you lock your doors and windows to keep your house secure, maintenance therapy helps keep your body safe from cancer. It's important to keep up with these treatments to make sure the cancer doesn't come back.</s>
easy_definition: Treatment that is given to help keep cancer from coming back after it has disappeared following the initial therapy. It may include treatment with drugs, vaccines, or antibodies that kill cancer cells, and it may be given for a long time.

formal_definition: performance status is A measure of how well a patient is able to perform ordinary tasks and carry out daily activities.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of "performance status" that's easy to understand:

Performance status is like a report card for your body. It measures how well you can do everyday things like walk, eat, or get dressed without feeling too tired or weak. Doctors use this test to see if you need more treatment or if you're getting better on their own. Imagine your body as a car, and performance status is like checking how fast it's going or if it's making any noise. If everything is running smoothly, your score will be high. But if you're feeling tired or having trouble moving around, your score might be lower.

In simpler terms, performance status is a way to check how well someone is doing in their daily life. Just like how you might have a grade point average (GPA) in school, doctors use performance status to keep track of how well their patients are doing overall.</s>
easy_definition: A rating of one’s ability to do daily activities.

formal_definition: trachea is The airway that leads from the larynx (voice box) to the bronchi (large airways that lead to the lungs). Also called windpipe.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of the term "trachea" for a non-medical person:

So, have you ever heard someone say "breathe into the mouthpiece" when they're checking your pulse or taking your blood pressure? That's because they're actually talking about your trachea! The trachea is like a long tube that connects your voice box (larynx) to your lungs. It's like a superlong straw that helps you breathe in and out. When doctors check your pulse or take your blood pressure, they're listening to the sound of blood flowing through your trachea. And if you've ever seen someone use a ventilator or get a tracheotomy, they're working with the trachea too! So, next time you hear someone talk about the trachea, you'll know exactly what they mean!</s>
easy_definition: The airway between the throat and airway into the lungs. Also called the windpipe.

formal_definition: anesthesia is A loss of feeling or awareness caused by drugs or other substances. Anesthesia keeps patients from feeling pain during surgery or other procedures. Local anesthesia is a loss of feeling in one small area of the body. Regional anesthesia is a loss of feeling in a part of the body, such as an arm or leg. General anesthesia is a loss of feeling and a complete loss of awareness that feels like a very deep sleep.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's how I would explain the term "anesthesia" to a non-medical person:

Anesthesia is a special kind of medicine that doctors use to make people feel numb or unaware of what's happening during a surgery or procedure. It's like a magic spell that takes away all the yucky feelings, so you don't feel any pain or discomfort. There are different kinds of anesthesia, like local anesthesia which only makes one tiny area feel numb, or regional anesthesia which makes a bigger area, like an arm or leg, feel numb. And then there's general anesthesia, which is like a really deep sleep, where you won't even remember the surgery happened. It's like a magic carpet ride to a pain-free world!</s>
easy_definition: A drug or other substance that causes a controlled loss of feeling or awareness with or without loss of wakefulness.

formal_definition: asymmetry is Lack or absence of balanced proportions between parts of a thing.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain the medical term "asymmetry" in a way that's easy to understand for a non-medical person:

Asymmetry means when something is not balanced or symmetrical. Imagine holding a pencil in front of your face - it's symmetrical, right? But if you hold it at an angle, so one end is higher than the other, it's no longer symmetrical. That's kind of like what asymmetry means in medicine. It means something isn't evenly balanced or proportionate on both sides of the body. For example, if one eye is bigger than the other, or if one arm is shorter than the other, that's asymmetry. It could be a problem with how the body grew during development, or it could be caused by an injury or disease. Either way, it's important to check it out with a doctor to figure out what's going on.</s>
easy_definition: One half or side of the mole does not match the other half or side.

formal_definition: Breslow thickness is A measure of how deeply a melanoma tumor has grown into the skin. The tumor thickness (depth) is usually measured from the top of the tumor to the deepest tumor cells. If the tumor is ulcerated (the skin is broken), it is measured from the base of the ulcer to the deepest tumor cells. Breslow thickness is used to help determine the stage of cancer. Thicker tumors are linked with lower survival rates. Also called Breslow depth.
simplified_explanation: Term 'Breslow thickness' not found in medical dictionary.
easy_definition: A measure of how deep the melanoma tumor has grown into the skin.

formal_definition: dermatologist is A doctor who has special training to diagnose and treat skin problems.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of what a dermatologist is:

A dermatologist is like a superhero for your skin! They have special training to help fix any problems with your skin, like acne, rashes, or even skin cancer. Just like how you might go to a doctor for a check-up to make sure your body is healthy, a dermatologist helps keep your skin healthy too. They use special tools and medicine to figure out what's causing the problem and make it better. So if you have any skin issues, don't worry – just see a dermatologist and they'll help you feel better in no time!</s>
easy_definition: A doctor who’s an expert in skin diseases.

formal_definition: dermis is The inner layer of the two main layers of the skin. The dermis has connective tissue, blood vessels, oil and sweat glands, nerves, hair follicles, and other structures. It is made up of a thin upper layer called the papillary dermis, and a thick lower layer called the reticular dermis.
simplified_explanation: Okay, let's break it down! The dermis is like the inside layer of your skin. Imagine it as a big sponge that's soft and squishy. Underneath that spongy layer, there are tiny blood vessels, oil and sweat glands, nerves, and even little hair follicles. These all work together to help keep your skin healthy and looking its best. Just like how you might use different ingredients in a recipe to make a delicious meal, the different parts of the dermis work together to create healthy skin. And just like how you need to take care of your sponge so it stays clean and works well, you need to take good care of your skin by washing it, moisturizing it, and protecting it from the sun to keep it happy and healthy!</s>
easy_definition: The second layer of skin that is beneath the top layer (epidermis).

formal_definition: distant metastasis is Refers to cancer that has spread from the original (primary) tumor to distant organs or distant lymph nodes. Also called distant cancer.
simplified_explanation:
Okay, here's my attempt:

Hey there! So, you know how cancer can grow and spread in your body? Well, sometimes it can travel to other parts of your body, like distant organs or lymph nodes, through something called "distant metastasis." It's like when you have a bunch of friends who all live in different places, and one of them gets a message to come visit you at your house. They might bring some of their friends along too. In the same way, cancer cells can break off from the primary tumor and travel to other parts of your body, creating new tumors. This is called distant metastasis, or distant cancer. It's not good news, because these new tumors can be harder to treat than the original ones. But doctors can still try to help you fight it with medicine and other treatments.</s>
easy_definition: Cancer cells have spread to a part of the body far away from the first (primary) melanoma tumor.

formal_definition: epidermis is The outer layer of the two main layers of the skin.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the term "epidermis" that's easy to understand and free of technical jargon:

The epidermis is like a protective outer layer on your skin. It's the topmost layer, right next to the air outside your body. Imagine it as a thin sheet that covers your entire body, protecting you from things like germs, irritants, and damage from the sun or wind. Just like how your clothes protect you from the cold or rain, the epidermis keeps your skin safe from harmful substances in the environment. It's made up of many tiny cells that work together to keep your skin healthy and strong.</s>
easy_definition: The outer layer of skin.

formal_definition: excisional biopsy is A surgical procedure in which a cut is made through the skin to remove an entire lump or suspicious area so it can be checked under a microscope for signs of disease. A small amount of healthy tissue around the abnormal area may also be removed.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain "excisional biopsy" in simple language:

An excision biopsy is like a surgical operation where a doctor makes a cut in your skin to take out a lump or a part of your body that doesn't look right. They take out the whole lump or area so they can check it under a microscope to see if it's sick. The doctor might also take out some of the good tissue around the bad area to make sure they get all of it. It's like cutting out a piece of bread with mold on it to make sure there's no mold left behind. The doctor wants to make sure they get all of the sick tissue so they can figure out what's making you sick.</s>
easy_definition: Surgery that removes the entire skin tumor or abnormal-looking area (lesion) to test for cancer cells.

formal_definition: general anesthesia is A temporary loss of feeling and a complete loss of awareness that feels like a very deep sleep. It is caused by special drugs or other substances called anesthetics. General anesthesia keeps patients from feeling pain during surgery or other procedures.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's how I would explain general anesthesia to a non-medical person:

General anesthesia is like a magic sleep that doctors use to make you feel completely unconscious during surgery or other medical procedures. It's like a powerful nap that makes you forget everything that's happening around you, so you don't feel any pain or discomfort. Just like when you're dreaming, you won't be able to see, hear, or feel anything while you're under anesthesia. And just like when you wake up from a good nap, you'll feel refreshed and ready to go! The special drugs or substances that do this magic are called anesthetics, and they work by blocking the signals between your brain and body, so you don't feel any pain or discomfort.</s>
easy_definition: A controlled loss of wakefulness from drugs.

formal_definition: infusion is A method of putting fluids, including drugs, into the bloodstream. Also called intravenous infusion.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my explanation of the term "infusion" for a non-medical person:

An infusion is when doctors or nurses put medicine or fluids directly into a patient's bloodstream through a special needle or tube. It's like giving someone a drink, but instead of water, they're getting medication or fluid straight into their body. This way, the medicine goes into the bloodstream faster and can start working sooner. It's often used when someone needs a quick boost of medicine or when they can't take it by mouth because their stomach isn't well enough. Just like how you might give a friend a drink of water or juice, an infusion is a way for doctors to give someone important medicine or fluids.</s>
easy_definition: A method of putting fluids, including drugs, into the bloodstream. Also called intravenous infusion.

formal_definition: lymphedema is A condition in which lymph builds up in tissues and causes swelling. Lymphedema usually affects an arm or leg, but it can also affect other parts of the body. Lymphedema can occur when lymph vessels or nodes become damaged or blocked, which affects the flow of lymph in the body. This is most commonly caused by cancer or cancer treatment, such as surgery or radiation therapy. It can also be caused by infection with certain parasites or by having certain inherited conditions in which the lymph nodes or lymph vessels are abnormal or absent. Lymphedema cannot be cured, but treatment can help relieve swelling, pain, and other symptoms.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain the term "lymphedema" in a simple and easy-to-understand way:

Lymphedema is like a traffic jam in your body. Imagine that your body has a special system called lymphatics that helps remove waste and extra fluids from your tissues. When something blocks this system, like a car accident on the highway, the waste and fluids can build up and cause swelling. This swelling can happen in any part of your body, like an arm or leg, but it can also happen in your chest or belly. Just like how you might feel uncomfortable or even get sick if you have a traffic jam on the highway, people with lymphedema can feel uncomfortable or have pain because of the swelling in their body. Unfortunately, there's no magic fix for lymphedema, but doctors can help manage the swelling with special treatments.</s>
easy_definition: Swelling due to buildup of lymph fluid in the fatty tissue just under the skin near the surgery site.

formal_definition: resectable is Able to be removed by surgery.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "resectable" to a non-medical person:

Resectable means that something can be removed or operated on through surgery. It's like when you need to fix a broken toy and you can take it apart and put it back together again. In medicine, doctors can use surgery to remove something that's causing problems, like a tumor or an injury, and they can do it in a way that leaves the rest of your body intact. So, if a doctor says that a patient's condition is resectable, it means that there's a good chance they can fix it with surgery.</s>
easy_definition: Cancer able to be removed (resected) by surgery.

formal_definition: ulceration is The formation of a break on the skin or on the surface of an organ. An ulcer forms when the surface cells die and are cast off. Ulcers may be associated with cancer and other diseases.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of the term "ulceration" in simple language:

An ulcer is like a small hole in your skin or organs. It happens when the top layers of your skin or organs get damaged and die, and then peel off. This can happen for many reasons, like from injury, infection, or even some diseases. When an ulcer forms, it can cause pain and discomfort, and sometimes it can lead to bigger problems if it's not treated. For example, if an ulcer forms on your stomach, it could bleed and cause vomiting blood, which can be very dangerous. So, it's important to see a doctor if you notice any unusual sores or holes on your body.</s>
easy_definition: A break in the skin like an open sore.

formal_definition: bone marrow is The soft, spongy tissue that has many blood vessels and is found in the center of most bones. There are two types of bone marrow: red and yellow. Red bone marrow contains blood stem cells that can become red blood cells, white blood cells, or platelets. Yellow bone marrow is made mostly of fat and contains stem cells that can become cartilage, fat, or bone cells.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt at explaining the term "bone marrow" in simple language:

Bone marrow is like a factory inside your bones. It's a soft, spongy stuff that has lots of tiny little factories called blood vessels. These factories make different types of cells that help keep you healthy. Inside these factories, there are special cells called stem cells that can turn into different types of cells, like red blood cells, white blood cells, or platelets. Red bone marrow makes more red blood cells than white blood cells or platelets, while yellow bone marrow makes mostly fat cells with some stem cells that can become other types of cells. Think of it like a mix of cookies and candy inside your bones - some are for making you feel good and strong, while others are just for fun!</s>
easy_definition: The soft, spongy tissue that has many blood vessels and is found in the center of most bones. There are two types of bone marrow: red and yellow. Red bone marrow contains blood stem cells that can become red blood cells, white blood cells, or platelets. Yellow bone marrow is made mostly of fat and contains stem cells that can become cartilage, fat, or bone cells.

formal_definition: bone marrow aspiration is A procedure in which a small sample of bone marrow is removed, usually from the hip bone, breastbone, or thigh bone. A small area of skin and the surface of the bone underneath are numbed with an anesthetic. Then, a special wide needle is pushed into the bone. A sample of liquid bone marrow is removed with a syringe attached to the needle. The bone marrow is sent to a laboratory to be looked at under a microscope. This procedure may be done at the same time as a bone marrow biopsy.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain "bone marrow aspiration" in simple language for a non-medical person:

Bone marrow aspiration is like taking a small sample of soup from a big pot. Imagine your body is like a big pot of soup, and inside that soup, there are tiny little things called cells that help keep you healthy. When a doctor does a bone marrow aspiration, they take a tiny spoonful of that soup to look at it more closely under a microscope. They do this to check if everything is working properly and to see if there might be any problems. It's kind of like checking the expiration date on a jar of jam - you want to make sure it's still good and not spoiled!</s>
easy_definition: A procedure in which a small sample of bone marrow is removed, usually from the hip bone, breastbone, or thigh bone. A small area of skin and the surface of the bone underneath are numbed with an anesthetic. Then, a special wide needle is pushed into the bone. A sample of liquid bone marrow is removed with a syringe attached to the needle. The bone marrow is sent to a laboratory to be looked at under a microscope. This procedure may be done at the same time as a bone marrow biopsy.

formal_definition: bone marrow biopsy is A procedure in which a small sample of bone with bone marrow inside it is removed, usually from the hip bone. A small area of skin and the surface of the bone underneath are numbed with an anesthetic. Then, a special, wide needle is pushed into the bone and rotated to remove a sample of bone with the bone marrow inside it. The sample is sent to a laboratory to be looked at under a microscope. This procedure may be done at the same time as a bone marrow aspiration.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining "bone marrow biopsy" in simple language for a non-medical person:

A bone marrow biopsy is like taking a small piece of cheese out of a big hunk of bread. The doctor uses a special needle to take a tiny bit of the soft, spongy tissue inside your bones that makes blood cells. They do this by numbing the area where the needle will go and then carefully inserting the needle to get the sample. This helps doctors see what's going on inside your body and figure out if there might be any problems with your blood cells. It's kind of like taking a quick peek inside your fridge to check if you have enough milk for your cereal.</s>
easy_definition: A procedure in which a small sample of bone with bone marrow inside it is removed, usually from the hip bone. A small area of skin and the surface of the bone underneath are numbed with an anesthetic. Then, a special, wide needle is pushed into the bone and rotated to remove a sample of bone with the bone marrow inside it. The sample is sent to a laboratory to be looked at under a microscope. This procedure may be done at the same time as a bone marrow aspiration.

formal_definition: gastroenterologist is A doctor who has special training in diagnosing and treating disorders of the digestive system.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain the term "gastroenterologist" in simple language for a non-medical person:

A gastroenterologist is a type of doctor who specializes in taking care of your tummy and digestive system. They have extra training to help diagnose and treat problems like stomachaches, diarrhea, constipation, and other issues that can make you feel uncomfortable or sick. Imagine having a friend who knows all about how to fix a leaky faucet or a broken leg - a gastroenterologist is like that for your digestive system! They can use special tools and tests to figure out what's going on inside your body and give you the best treatment plan to make you feel better. So if you ever have any tummy troubles, you can see a gastroenterologist for help.</s>
easy_definition: A doctor who’s an expert in digestive diseases.

formal_definition: histology is The study of tissues and cells under a microscope.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain histology to a non-medical person:

Histology is like taking a really close look at tiny building blocks of our bodies. It's like using a special tool called a microscope to see individual bricks (called cells) that make up bigger structures (like buildings or organs). By studying these tiny bricks, doctors and scientists can learn how they work together to keep us healthy and figure out why things might go wrong when they don't. It's like solving a puzzle, but instead of pieces, we're looking at single cells!</s>
easy_definition: The study of tissues and cells under a microscope.

formal_definition: immunophenotyping is A process that uses antibodies to identify cells based on the types of antigens or markers on the surface of the cells. This process is used in basic research and to help diagnose diseases, such as specific types of leukemia and lymphoma. Immunophenotyping may also be used to separate cells into different groups based on the markers they have on the surface.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to explain immunophenotyping in a way that's easy to understand!

Immunophenotyping is like a special kind of detective work for doctors. They use tiny particles called antibodies to find out what's on the surface of cells in your body.
