formal_definition: adenoma is A tumor that is not cancer. It starts in gland-like cells of the epithelial tissue (thin layer of tissue that covers organs, glands, and other structures within the body).
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain the term "adenoma" in simple language:

An adenoma is like a small bump on a surface. Imagine you have a smooth, flat surface, like a piece of paper. Now imagine there's a small, round bump on it. That's kind of like an adenoma - it's a small growth that forms on the surface of your body, usually in the lining of an organ or gland. Unlike cancer, which is a more serious and invasive growth, adenomas are generally harmless and don't spread to other parts of the body. They're like small, benign bumps that can be removed with minimal treatment.</s>
easy_definition: A tumor that is not cancer. It starts in gland-like cells of the epithelial tissue (thin layer of tissue that covers organs, glands, and other structures within the body).

formal_definition: adrenocortical carcinoma is A rare cancer that forms in the outer layer of tissue of the adrenal gland (a small organ on top of each kidney that makes steroid hormones, adrenaline, and noradrenaline to control heart rate, blood pressure, and other body functions). Also called adrenocortical cancer and cancer of the adrenal cortex.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining "adrenocortical carcinoma" in simple language:

Adrenocortical carcinoma is a type of cancer that starts in the outer layer of the adrenal gland. Your adrenal glands are like tiny superheroes inside your body that help regulate things like your heart rate, blood pressure, and how much energy you have. Sometimes, bad cells can grow inside one of these glands and turn into cancer. It's a rare kind of cancer, but it's important to know about it so we can help people who might have it. Imagine your adrenal gland as a special kind of garden, and the bad cells are like weeds that need to be pulled out before they take over the whole thing. With the right treatment, we can help keep those weeds under control and keep your body healthy!</s>
easy_definition: A rare cancer that forms in the outer layer of tissue of the adrenal gland (a small organ on top of each kidney that makes steroid hormones, adrenaline, and noradrenaline to control heart rate, blood pressure, and other body functions). Also called adrenocortical cancer and cancer of the adrenal cortex.

formal_definition: stage IV prostate cancer is Stage IV is divided into stages IVA and IVB. In stage IVA, cancer is found in one or both sides of the prostate and may have spread to the seminal vesicles or to nearby tissue or organs, such as the rectum, bladder, or pelvic wall. Cancer has spread to nearby lymph nodes. The prostate-specific antigen (PSA) can be any level and the Grade Group is 1, 2, 3, 4, or 5 (Gleason score is 10 or less). In stage IVB, cancer has spread to other parts of the body, such as the bones or distant lymph nodes.
simplified_explanation: Term 'stage IV prostate cancer' not found in medical dictionary.
easy_definition: Stage IV is divided into stages IVA and IVB. In stage IVA, cancer is found in one or both sides of the prostate and may have spread to the seminal vesicles or to nearby tissue or organs, such as the rectum, bladder, or pelvic wall. Cancer has spread to nearby lymph nodes. The prostate-specific antigen (PSA) can be any level and the Grade Group is 1, 2, 3, 4, or 5 (Gleason score is 10 or less). In stage IVB, cancer has spread to other parts of the body, such as the bones or distant lymph nodes.

formal_definition: aggressive is In medicine, describes a tumor or disease that forms, grows, or spreads quickly. It may also describe treatment that is more severe or intense than usual.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "aggressive" in medicine to a non-medical person:

In medicine, the term "aggressive" refers to something that grows or spreads very quickly. For example, an aggressive tumor is one that grows faster than usual and can spread to other parts of the body. Similarly, aggressive treatment might be more intense or severe than usual, like chemotherapy that targets cancer cells more strongly than standard treatment. Just like how a fire can quickly spread through a forest, an aggressive disease or treatment can move quickly through the body. So, when doctors say something is aggressive, they mean it's moving fast and could potentially cause problems if left unchecked.</s>
easy_definition: In medicine, describes a tumor or disease that forms, grows, or spreads quickly.

formal_definition: AJCC staging system is A system to describe the amount and spread of cancer in a patient’s body, using TNM. T describes the size of the tumor and any spread of cancer into nearby tissue; N describes spread of cancer to nearby lymph nodes; and M describes metastasis (spread of cancer to other parts of the body). This system was created and is updated by the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) and the International Union Against Cancer (UICC). The AJCC staging system is used to describe most types of cancer. Also called TNM staging system.
simplified_explanation: Term 'AJCC staging system' not found in medical dictionary.
easy_definition: A system to describe the amount and spread of cancer in a patient’s body, using TNM. T describes the size of the tumor and any spread of cancer into nearby tissue; N describes spread of cancer to nearby lymph nodes; and M describes metastasis (spread of cancer to other parts of the body). This system was created and is updated by the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) and the International Union Against Cancer (UICC). The AJCC staging system is used to describe most types of cancer. Also called TNM staging system.

formal_definition: allergic response is A hypersensitive immune reaction to a substance that normally is harmless or would not cause an immune response in most people. An allergic response may cause harmful symptoms such as itching or inflammation or tissue injury.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt at explaining the term "allergic response" in simple language for a non-medical person:

Hey there! So, have you ever heard of something called an allergy? It's when your body gets super sensitive to something like pollen or peanuts, and it thinks it's under attack. Your body then tries to fight back by releasing chemicals that can cause problems like sneezing, itchiness, or even swelling. This is called an allergic response. It's kind of like when you touch something hot and your skin gets red and irritated - it's the same idea, just on a bigger scale. And sometimes, these allergic responses can be really dangerous, so it's important to know what might trigger them and how to stay safe.</s>
easy_definition: A hypersensitive immune reaction to a substance that normally is harmless or would not cause an immune response in most people. An allergic response may cause harmful symptoms such as itching or inflammation or tissue injury.

formal_definition: allogeneic is Taken from different individuals of the same species. Also called allogenic.
simplified_explanation:
Alright! Here's my attempt to explain the term "allogeneic" in simple language:

Hey there! So, you know how sometimes we share things with our friends or family members? Like a book, a toy, or even food? Well, in medicine, we have something similar called "allogenic." It means taking cells, tissues, or organs from one person and giving them to another person who needs them. It's like sharing resources between friends - instead of just sharing toys, we're sharing parts of ourselves to help someone else feel better. Just like how you might share your favorite toy with a friend who really wants it, doctors can share cells or tissues with someone who needs them to fix an injury or sickness.</s>

formal_definition: allogeneic stem cell transplant is A procedure in which a patient receives healthy blood-forming cells (stem cells) from a donor to replace their own stem cells that have been destroyed by treatment with radiation or high doses of chemotherapy. In an allogeneic stem cell transplant, the healthy stem cells may come from the blood or bone marrow of a related donor who is not an identical twin of the patient or from an unrelated donor who is genetically similar to the patient. An allogeneic stem cell transplant is most often used to treat blood cancers, such as leukemia and lymphoma, and certain types of blood or immune system disorders.

The doctor will give you healthy stem cells from someone else's body (a donor) to help fix the damage and get your factory back up and running again. These stem cells come from either a related donor (like a sibling or parent) or an unrelated donor who is similar to you. formal_definition: androgen deprivation is Treatment to suppress or block the production or action of male hormones. This is done by having the testicles removed, by taking female sex hormones, or by taking drugs called antiandrogens. Also called 44369 and androgen ablation.

This can help slow down the growth of the cancer cells. There are different ways to do this, like surgery to remove the testicles, taking certain medicines called antiandrogens, or even giving estrogen to women. The goal is to reduce the amount of male hormones in the body to help fight the cancer.</s>

formal_definition: antiandrogen is A substance that keeps androgens (male sex hormones) from binding to proteins called androgen receptors, which are found in normal prostate cells, some prostate cancer cells, and in cells of some other tissues. Preventing this binding blocks the effects of these hormones in the body. Treatment with antiandrogens may keep prostate cancer cells from growing. Examples of antiandrogens used to treat prostate cancer are apalutamide, bicalutamide, darolutamide, enzalutamide, flutamide, and nilutamide. Also called androgen receptor antagonist and androgen receptor blocker.

formal_definition: antibody is A protein made by plasma cells (a type of white blood cell) in response to an antigen (a substance that causes the body to make a specific immune response). Each antibody can bind to only one specific antigen. The purpose of this binding is to help destroy the antigen. Some antibodies destroy antigens directly. Others make it easier for white blood cells to destroy the antigen. An antibody is a type of immunoglobulin.

formal_definition: antiestrogen is A substance that keeps cells from making or using estrogen (a hormone that plays a role in female sex characteristics, the menstrual cycle, and pregnancy). An antiestrogen is a type of hormone antagonist. Also called estrogen blocker.

An antiestrogen works by stopping estrogen from doing its job in certain cells, which can help keep cancer from growing. formal_definition: aromatase inhibitor is A drug that blocks the activity of an enzyme called aromatase, which the body uses to make estrogen in the ovaries and other tissues. Blocking aromatase lowers the amount of estrogen made by the body, which may stop the growth of cancer cells that need estrogen to grow. Aromatase inhibitors are used to treat some types of breast cancer or to keep it from coming back. They may also be used to help prevent breast cancer in some women who are at a high risk of developing it. Examples of aromatase inhibitors are anastrozole, letrozole, and exemestane. Aromatase inhibitors are a type of hormone therapy. Also called AI drug.

Autologous means something that comes from your own body. It's like when you have a piece of candy and you give it back to yourself. The same thing happens with cells or tissues in medicine. Doctors take some of your own cells or tissues and use them to fix or replace something else in your body. For example, they might take some of your skin cells and use them to cover up a burn. formal_definition: autologous stem cell transplant is A procedure in which a patient’s healthy stem cells (blood-forming cells) are collected from the blood or bone marrow before treatment, stored, and then given back to the patient after treatment. An autologous stem cell transplant replaces a patient’s stem cells that were destroyed by treatment with radiation or high doses of chemotherapy. An autologous stem cell transplant is most often used to treat blood cancers, such as leukemia and lymphoma.

formal_definition: axillary lymph node dissection is Surgery to remove lymph nodes found in the armpit region. Also called axillary dissection.

formal_definition: biosimilar drug is A biological drug that is very much like another biological drug (called the reference drug) that has already been approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA). Biosimilar drugs and reference drugs are made from living organisms but they may be made in different ways and of slightly different substances. To be called a biosimilar drug, a biological drug must be shown to be as safe as, work as well as, and work in the same way as its reference drug. It must also be used in the same way, at the same dose, and for the same condition as the reference drug. Biosimilar drugs must be approved by FDA, and may cost less than the reference drugs.

Both the original medicine and the biosimilar drug have to work the same way and be just as safe and effective, but the biosimilar drug might be a little cheaper. So, it's like getting a similar treat at a lower price!</s>

formal_definition: bone mineral density is A measure of the amount of minerals (mostly calcium and phosphorous) contained in a certain volume of bone. Bone mineral density measurements are used to diagnose osteoporosis (a condition marked by decreased bone mass), to see how well osteoporosis treatments are working, and to predict how likely the bones are to break. Low bone mineral density can occur in patients treated for cancer. Also called BMD, bone density, and bone mass.

formal_definition: breast-conserving surgery is Surgery to remove cancer or other abnormal tissue from the breast and some normal tissue around it, but not the breast itself. formal_definition: grade is In cancer, a description of a tumor based on how abnormal the cancer cells and tissue look under a microscope and how quickly the cancer cells are likely to grow and spread. formal_definition: screening is Checking for disease when there are no symptoms. Since screening may find diseases at an early stage, there may be a better chance of curing the disease. Screening can also include doing a genetic test to check for a person’s risk of developing an inherited disease.

They use special tests like mammograms, colonoscopies, Pap tests, and genetic tests to look for any signs of disease. If they find something, they can treat it early on and help prevent bigger problems from happening. It's like getting a tune-up for your body!</s>
easy_definition: Checking for disease when there are no symptoms. Since screening may find diseases at an early stage, there may be a better chance of curing the disease. Examples of cancer screening tests are the mammogram (for breast cancer), colonoscopy (for colon cancer), and the Pap test and HPV tests (for cervical cancer). Screening can also include doing a genetic test to check for a person’s risk of developing an inherited disease.

formal_definition: CAPOX is An abbreviation for a chemotherapy combination used to treat advanced colorectal cancer. It is also being studied in the treatment of other types of cancer. It includes the drugs capecitabine and oxaliplatin. Also called CAPOX regimen.
simplified_explanation: Term 'CAPOX' not found in medical dictionary.
easy_definition: An abbreviation for a chemotherapy combination used to treat advanced colorectal cancer. It is also being studied in the treatment of other types of cancer. It includes the drugs capecitabine and oxaliplatin. Also called CAPOX regimen.

formal_definition: carcinoembryonic antigen is A substance that may be found in the blood of people who have colon cancer, other types of cancer or diseases, or who smoke tobacco. Carcinoembryonic antigen levels may help keep track of how well cancer treatments are working or if cancer has come back. It is a type of tumor marker. Also called CEA.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to explain "carcinoembryonic antigen" in simple terms!

Carcinoembryonic antigen is like a special kind of protein that is found in your body. It's like a tag on a toy that helps doctors figure out if something is going wrong with your body. When someone has cancer, like colon cancer, their body makes too much of this protein, so it shows up in their blood test results. Doctors can use these tests to see if cancer treatments are working or if the cancer comes back. It's like a report card for your body's health!</s>
easy_definition: A substance that may be found in the blood of people who have colon cancer, other types of cancer or diseases, or who smoke tobacco. Carcinoembryonic antigen levels may help keep track of how well cancer treatments are working or if cancer has come back. It is a type of tumor marker. Also called CEA.

formal_definition: carcinoma in situ is A condition in which abnormal cells that look like cancer cells under a microscope are found only in the place where they first formed and haven’t spread to nearby tissue. At some point, these cells may become cancerous and spread into nearby normal tissue. There are many different types of carcinoma in situ depending on the type of tissue in which it began. These include adenocarcinoma in situ (of the cervix, lungs, and gastrointestinal tract), ductal carcinoma in situ (of the breast), and squamous cell carcinoma in situ (of the skin, mouth, and larynx). Also called stage 0 disease.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain carcinoma in situ in simple language:

Imagine your body is like a garden with beautiful flowers and trees. Sometimes, some of those flowers or trees might grow in a way that looks a little weird or different from the others. Maybe they're growing too fast or in an unusual shape. That's kind of like what happens when you have carcinoma in situ. It's when some of the cells in your body start to grow abnormally and look like they could turn into cancer. But the good news is, if we catch it early, it hasn't spread to other parts of your body yet, so we can try to get rid of those extra growths before they become a bigger problem. There are different types of carcinoma in situ depending on where it starts in your body, like in the cervix, breast, or skin. So, it's important to go to the doctor for check-ups to make sure everything is okay!</s>
easy_definition: A condition in which abnormal cells that look like cancer cells under a microscope are found only in the place where they first formed and haven’t spread to nearby tissue. At some point, these cells may become cancerous and spread into nearby normal tissue. There are many different types of carcinoma in situ depending on the type of tissue in which it began. These include adenocarcinoma in situ (of the cervix, lungs, and gastrointestinal tract), ductal carcinoma in situ (of the breast), and squamous cell carcinoma in situ (of the skin, mouth, and larynx). Also called stage 0 disease.

formal_definition: castration is Removal or destruction of the testicles or ovaries using radiation, surgery, or drugs. Medical castration refers to the use of drugs to suppress the function of the ovaries or testicles.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of the term "castration" for a non-medical person:

Castration is when doctors remove or stop the working parts of your reproductive system, like the testicles or ovaries. It's like taking away the batteries that make your body produce eggs or sperm. Doctors can use different methods to do this, like medicine, surgery, or radiation. For example, if someone has cancer in their ovaries, doctors might give them medicine to stop the cancer from growing. Or, they might cut out the ovaries during surgery. Castration can help treat some health problems, but it can also have side effects, like changing how your body looks or feels.</s>
easy_definition: Removal or destruction of the testicles or ovaries using radiation, surgery, or drugs. Medical castration refers to the use of drugs to suppress the function of the ovaries or testicles.

formal_definition: cervical intraepithelial neoplasia is A term used to describe abnormal cells that are found on the surface of the cervix when a biopsy is done. Cervical intraepithelial neoplasia is usually caused by infection with certain types of human papillomavirus (HPV). Cervical intraepithelial neoplasia is not cancer but may become cancer and spread to nearby normal tissue. It is graded on a scale of 1 to 3, based on how abnormal the cells look under a microscope and how much of the cervical tissue is affected. For example, CIN 1 has slightly abnormal cells and is less likely to become cancer than CIN 2 or CIN 3. Cervical intraepithelial neoplasia is sometimes called cervical dysplasia. Also called CIN.
simplified_explanation: Okay, let me explain cervical intraepithelial neoplasia in a way that's easy to understand! So, you know how your body has different layers, like an onion? Well, the cervix is like the bottom layer of that onion, and it's where babies grow inside the mom's belly during pregnancy. Sometimes, there can be some abnormal cells growing on the surface of the cervix, kind of like a weird spot on an apple. These abnormal cells are called cervical intraepithelial neoplasia, or CIN for short. They're not cancer, but they can turn into cancer if they're not treated. Doctors use a number system to grade how bad the abnormal cells are, like how many fingers you hold up to show how many chocolate chip cookies you want. The lower the number, the less bad the cells are. So, CIN 1 is like having a few crumbs on your shirt, while CIN 3 is like having a big ol' cookie stain! And just like you need to clean your shirt, doctors might need to remove those abnormal cells to keep your body healthy.</s>
easy_definition: A term used to describe abnormal cells that are found on the surface of the cervix when a biopsy is done. Cervical intraepithelial neoplasia is usually caused by infection with certain types of human papillomavirus (HPV). Cervical intraepithelial neoplasia is not cancer but may become cancer and spread to nearby normal tissue. It is graded on a scale of 1 to 3, based on how abnormal the cells look under a microscope and how much of the cervical tissue is affected. For example, CIN 1 has slightly abnormal cells and is less likely to become cancer than CIN 2 or CIN 3. Cervical intraepithelial neoplasia is sometimes called cervical dysplasia. Also called CIN.

formal_definition: CAR T-cell therapy is A type of treatment in which a patient's T cells (a type of immune system cell) are changed in the laboratory so they will attack cancer cells. T cells are taken from a patient’s blood. Then the gene for a special receptor that binds to a certain protein on the patient’s cancer cells is added to the T cells in the laboratory. The special receptor is called a chimeric antigen receptor (CAR). Large numbers of the CAR T cells are grown in the laboratory and given to the patient by infusion. CAR T-cell therapy is used to treat certain blood cancers, and it is being studied in the treatment of other types of cancer. Also called chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy.
simplified_explanation: Term 'CAR T-cell therapy' not found in medical dictionary.
easy_definition: A type of treatment in which a patient's T cells (a type of immune system cell) are changed in the laboratory so they will attack cancer cells. T cells are taken from a patient’s blood. Then the gene for a special receptor that binds to a certain protein on the patient’s cancer cells is added to the T cells in the laboratory. The special receptor is called a chimeric antigen receptor (CAR). Large numbers of the CAR T cells are grown in the laboratory and given to the patient by infusion. CAR T-cell therapy is used to treat certain blood cancers, and it is being studied in the treatment of other types of cancer. Also called chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy.

formal_definition: chronic obstructive pulmonary disease is A type of lung disease marked by permanent damage to tissues in the lungs, making it hard to breathe. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease includes chronic bronchitis, in which the bronchi (large air passages) are inflamed and scarred, and emphysema, in which the alveoli (tiny air sacs) are damaged. It develops over many years and is usually caused by cigarette smoking. Also called COPD.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) in simple language for a non-medical person:

COPD is a serious lung disease that makes it hard to breathe. Imagine your lungs are like a garden with beautiful flowers. When you have COPD, those flowers get old and die, making it harder for air to get through. It's like there's a bunch of weeds growing inside your lungs, blocking the way for fresh air to reach your body. And just like how you might need to water and take care of a garden, people with COPD need special treatments to help their lungs work better. Unfortunately, COPD often starts from smoking cigarettes, so quitting can really help improve breathing again.</s>
easy_definition: A type of lung disease marked by permanent damage to tissues in the lungs, making it hard to breathe. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease includes chronic bronchitis, in which the bronchi (large air passages) are inflamed and scarred, and emphysema, in which the alveoli (tiny air sacs) are damaged. It develops over many years and is usually caused by cigarette smoking. Also called COPD.

formal_definition: clinical breast exam is A physical exam of the breast performed by a health care provider to check for lumps or other changes. Also called CBE.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's how I would explain "clinical breast exam" in simple language:

A clinical breast exam is when a doctor or nurse checks your breasts for any changes, like lumps or abnormal feelings. It's like giving your breasts a little hug and checking if everything feels normal. They might also look for any unusual marks or shapes on your skin. Just like how you check yourself for any changes in your body, like a rash or a sore throat, a clinical breast exam is when a professional does it for you. It's important because it can help find any problems early on, which makes it easier to treat them.</s>
easy_definition: A physical exam of the breast performed by a health care provider to check for lumps or other changes. Also called CBE.

formal_definition: colectomy is An operation to remove all or part of the colon. When only part of the colon is removed, it is called a partial colectomy. In an open colectomy, one long incision is made in the wall of the abdomen and doctors can see the colon directly. In a laparoscopic-assisted colectomy, several small incisions are made and a thin, lighted tube attached to a video camera is inserted through one opening to guide the surgery. Surgical instruments are inserted through the other openings to perform the surgery.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt at explaining "colectomy" in simple language for a non-medical person:

A colectomy is like a cleaning of your tummy. The doctor removes all or part of your colon, which is like a long, hollow tube inside your belly that helps digest food. If you have a problem with your colon, like a blockage or a bad ulcer, the doctor might need to take it out to fix the problem. They can do this through one big cut in your belly (open colectomy) or through several smaller cuts with special tools (laparoscopic-assisted colectomy). Either way, the goal is to get rid of the bad part and help your body heal.</s>
easy_definition: An operation to remove all or part of the colon. When only part of the colon is removed, it is called a partial colectomy. In an open colectomy, one long incision is made in the wall of the abdomen and doctors can see the colon directly. In a laparoscopic-assisted colectomy, several small incisions are made and a thin, lighted tube attached to a video camera is inserted through one opening to guide the surgery. Surgical instruments are inserted through the other openings to perform the surgery.

formal_definition: colonoscopy is Examination of the inside of the colon using a colonoscope, inserted into the rectum. A colonoscope is a thin, tube-like instrument with a light and a lens for viewing. It may also have a tool to remove tissue to be checked under a microscope for signs of disease.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the term "colonoscopy" that's easy to understand:

A colonoscopy is like a magic wand that doctors use to look inside your tummy. They insert a long, thin tube called a colonoscope through your bottom so they can see inside your colon (which is like a long, hollow tube inside your body). The colonoscope has a special light and lens that helps them see any problems or diseases inside your colon. Sometimes, they might even take tiny pieces of your colon to look at under a microscope to check for signs of disease. It's like a mini-vacation for your colon! 😊</s>
easy_definition: Examination of the inside of the colon using a colonoscope, inserted into the rectum. A colonoscope is a thin, tube-like instrument with a light and a lens for viewing. It may also have a tool to remove tissue to be checked under a microscope for signs of disease.

formal_definition: colostomy is An opening into the colon from the outside of the body. A colostomy provides a new path for waste material to leave the body after part of the colon has been removed.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining the term "colostomy" in simple language:

A colostomy is like a detour for your body's waste. When some of your colon is removed, the doctors create a new opening on the outside of your body that leads directly to the inside of your colon. This new opening allows waste to leave your body through this new path instead of going through your regular colon. It's kind of like when you take a detour on a road trip - you still reach your destination, but you go through a different route than you normally would. In this case, the waste goes through a different route out of your body.

I hope that helps! Let me know if you have any other questions.</s>
easy_definition: An opening into the colon from the outside of the body. A colostomy provides a new path for waste material to leave the body after part of the colon has been removed.

formal_definition: combination therapy is Therapy that combines more than one method of treatment. Also called multimodality therapy and multimodality treatment.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of "combination therapy" in simple language for a non-medical person:

Combination therapy means using two or more different treatments together to help someone feel better. It's like having a team of superheroes working together to fight off a bad guy. Each hero has their own special powers, and they work together to defeat the villain. In medicine, we use different treatments like medications, surgery, or radiation to attack the bad guys in our body, like cancer cells. By combining these treatments, we can make them stronger and more effective, just like how the superheroes work together to save the day. So, combination therapy is when we use multiple methods to give the best chance of success.</s>
easy_definition: Therapy that combines more than one method of treatment. Also called multimodality therapy and multimodality treatment.

formal_definition: complete blood count is A measure of the number of red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets in the blood. The amount of hemoglobin (substance in the blood that carries oxygen) and the hematocrit (the amount of whole blood that is made up of red blood cells) are also measured. A complete blood count is used to help diagnose and monitor many conditions. Also called blood cell count, CBC, and full blood count.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain "complete blood count" in simple language:

A complete blood count is like a report card for your blood. It shows how many red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets you have, as well as how much hemoglobin (a special protein that carries oxygen) and hematocrit (the percentage of your blood that's made up of red blood cells). This test helps doctors figure out if something's wrong with your body, like an infection or a lack of oxygen. It's like a checkup for your blood!</s>
easy_definition: A measure of the number of red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets in the blood. The amount of hemoglobin (substance in the blood that carries oxygen) and the hematocrit (the amount of whole blood that is made up of red blood cells) are also measured. A complete blood count is used to help diagnose and monitor many conditions. Also called blood cell count, CBC, and full blood count.

formal_definition: complete response is The disappearance of all signs of cancer in response to treatment. This does not always mean the cancer has been cured. Also called complete remission.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "complete response" in a way that's easy to understand for a non-medical person:

Imagine you have a toy box full of blocks. Each block represents a cancer cell in your body. If you take all the blocks out of the box, it means that all the cancer cells are gone and there are no more signs of cancer left. That's kind of like what it means when we say someone has a complete response to treatment. It doesn't necessarily mean that the cancer will never come back, but for now, it's completely gone. So, it's like taking all the blocks out of the toy box and putting them away forever. Just remember, even though the blocks are gone, it doesn't mean the toy box is empty forever – sometimes new blocks can appear, which is why doctors still need to monitor their patients closely after treatment.</s>
easy_definition: The disappearance of all signs of cancer in response to treatment. This does not always mean the cancer has been cured. Also called complete remission.

formal_definition: lymph node dissection is A surgical procedure in which the lymph nodes are removed and a sample of tissue is checked under a microscope for signs of cancer. For a regional lymph node dissection, some of the lymph nodes in the tumor area are removed; for a radical lymph node dissection, most or all of the lymph nodes in the tumor area are removed. Also called lymphadenectomy.
simplified_explanation: Lymph node dissection is like a cleaning crew for your body's defense system. It's a surgery where doctors remove some or all of the lymph nodes near a tumor to check if there are any bad cells inside them. Imagine your body has a network of tiny soldiers called lymphocytes that fight off germs and disease. If those soldiers get overwhelmed by an invading virus or cancer cell, they might not be able to fight effectively. The lymph node dissection helps doctors take out the infected soldiers and check if there are any more bad cells nearby that need to be removed. This way, the rest of the body's defense system can work better and keep you healthy!</s>
easy_definition: A surgical procedure in which the lymph nodes are removed and a sample of tissue is checked under a microscope for signs of cancer. For a regional lymph node dissection, some of the lymph nodes in the tumor area are removed; for a radical lymph node dissection, most or all of the lymph nodes in the tumor area are removed. Also called lymphadenectomy.

formal_definition: computed tomography scan is A procedure that uses a computer linked to an x-ray machine to make a series of detailed pictures of areas inside the body. The pictures are taken from different angles and are used to create 3-dimensional (3-D) views of tissues and organs. A dye may be injected into a vein or swallowed to help the tissues and organs show up more clearly. A computed tomography scan may be used to help diagnose disease, plan treatment, or find out how well treatment is working. Also called CAT scan, computerized axial tomography scan, computerized tomography, and CT scan.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain "computed tomography scan" in simple language:

A computed tomography scan is like a 3D movie of your insides. It takes a bunch of X-ray pictures from different angles and puts them together to create a super detailed picture of your tissues and organs. Sometimes, a special dye is added to help the pictures come out even clearer. This test can help doctors figure out if something is wrong, plan the best way to treat it, or see how well treatment is working. It's kind of like having a magic wand that shows doctors exactly what's going on inside your body!</s>
easy_definition: A procedure that uses a computer linked to an x-ray machine to make a series of detailed pictures of areas inside the body. The pictures are taken from different angles and are used to create 3-dimensional (3-D) views of tissues and organs. A dye may be injected into a vein or swallowed to help the tissues and organs show up more clearly. A computed tomography scan may be used to help diagnose disease, plan treatment, or find out how well treatment is working. Also called CAT scan, computerized axial tomography scan, computerized tomography, and CT scan.

formal_definition: continent reservoir is A pouch formed from a piece of small intestine to hold urine after the bladder has been removed.
simplified_explanation:
Okay, here's my attempt at explaining "continent reservoir" in simple language:

A continent reservoir is like a special container that doctors create to help people who no longer have a bladder. When someone loses their bladder, they may not be able to control their pee as well as they used to. So, the doctor will take a little bit of the small intestine and turn it into a pouch that can hold the pee. This pouch is called a continent reservoir because it helps keep the pee inside the body, rather than having it leak out. It's kind of like a superhero cape for your pee!</s>
easy_definition: A pouch formed from a piece of small intestine to hold urine after the bladder has been removed.

formal_definition: contrast material is A dye or other substance that helps show abnormal areas inside the body. It is given by injection into a vein, by enema, or by mouth. Contrast material may be used with x-rays, CT scans, MRI, or other imaging tests.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Let me explain what a contrast material is using easy-to-understand language and relatable examples:

So, have you ever taken a picture of yourself without any filters on your phone? The resulting image might look a bit fuzzy or washed out. Now imagine taking that same picture, but this time adding some special effects to make it stand out more - like making the colors pop or adding a cool filter. That's kind of like what contrast material does for our bodies when we get an imaging test done. It helps doctors see inside us more clearly by highlighting certain parts, almost like adding a special effect to a photo. They can use it during X-rays, CT scans, MRIs, or other imaging tests to find out if there's anything going on inside us that they couldn't see before. Just like how a filter can make a picture look better, contrast material helps doctors see things more clearly inside our bodies.</s>

formal_definition: cytogenetics is The study of chromosomes, which are long strands of DNA and protein that contain most of the genetic information in a cell. Cytogenetics involves testing samples of tissue, blood, or bone marrow in a laboratory to look for changes in chromosomes, including broken, missing, rearranged, or extra chromosomes. Changes in certain chromosomes may be a sign of a genetic disease or condition or some types of cancer. Cytogenetics may be used to help diagnose a disease or condition, plan treatment, or find out how well treatment is working.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to explain cytogenetics in a way that's easy to understand!

Cytogenetics is like taking a close-up picture of your cells. Just like how a photographer takes a picture of a person or object to see their details, a cytogeneticist takes a close look at the inside of a cell to see its chromosomes. Chromosomes are like the instruction booklets inside the cell that tell it how to grow and work properly. If there are any changes in these instructions, it could mean there's something wrong with the cell or the person it belongs to. By studying these changes, doctors can figure out what might be causing a problem and come up with ways to fix it.

Think of it like this: Imagine you have a recipe book filled with all the instructions for making your favorite meal. Each recipe has a set number of ingredients and steps to follow. Now imagine one of the ingredients is missing or the steps are out of order - the dish might not turn out right. That's kind of like what happens when there are changes in the chromosomes inside a cell. The cell might not be able to function properly because of those changes.</s>
easy_definition: The study of chromosomes, which are long strands of DNA and protein that contain most of the genetic information in a cell. Cytogenetics involves testing samples of tissue, blood, or bone marrow in a laboratory to look for changes in chromosomes, including broken, missing, rearranged, or extra chromosomes. Changes in certain chromosomes may be a sign of a genetic disease or condition or some types of cancer. Cytogenetics may be used to help diagnose a disease or condition, plan treatment, or find out how well treatment is working.

formal_definition: cytokine release syndrome is A condition that may occur after treatment with some types of immunotherapy, such as monoclonal antibodies and CAR-T cells. Cytokine release syndrome is caused by a large, rapid release of cytokines into the blood from immune cells affected by the immunotherapy. Cytokines are immune substances that have many different actions in the body. Signs and symptoms of cytokine release syndrome include fever, nausea, headache, rash, rapid heartbeat, low blood pressure, and trouble breathing. Most patients have a mild reaction, but sometimes, the reaction may be severe or life threatening.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain cytokine release syndrome in simple language:

Cytokine release syndrome is like a party in your body where immune cells are releasing lots of messages, called cytokines, into your bloodstream. These messages tell other parts of your immune system what to do, like fight off bad guys or repair damaged tissues. But sometimes, too many messages get sent out all at once, and it can cause problems like fevers, nausea, and trouble breathing. It's like when too many guests show up at a party uninvited - things can get out of hand quickly! Most people don't have any serious issues, but in rare cases, it can be really dangerous.</s>
easy_definition: A condition that may occur after treatment with some types of immunotherapy, such as monoclonal antibodies and CAR-T cells. Cytokine release syndrome is caused by a large, rapid release of cytokines into the blood from immune cells affected by the immunotherapy. Cytokines are immune substances that have many different actions in the body. Signs and symptoms of cytokine release syndrome include fever, nausea, headache, rash, rapid heartbeat, low blood pressure, and trouble breathing. Most patients have a mild reaction, but sometimes, the reaction may be severe or life threatening.

formal_definition: deoxyribonucleic acid is The molecule inside cells that contains the genetic information needed for a person and most other organisms to develop and grow and is passed from one generation to the next. Deoxyribonucleic acid is made up of two strands that twist into the shape of a spiral ladder called a double helix. Each strand has a backbone that is made up of sugar and phosphate molecules that attach to one of four bases: adenine (A), thymine (T), guanine (G), and cytosine (C). The bases pair up with one another (A with T, and G with C) to form chemical bonds, which act like rungs on a ladder. This holds the two strands of deoxyribonucleic acid together. Also called DNA.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) in simple language:

DNA is like a blueprint for your body. It's like a special set of instructions that are inside every cell in your body, telling them how to grow and work properly. These instructions are made up of four different letters - A, C, G, and T - that are arranged in a special code. Imagine each letter as a rung on a ladder, and when you put them all together, they form a long, twisted ladder called a double helix. This ladder holds all the secrets of who you are and what makes you unique! Just like a recipe book has instructions for making a delicious meal, your DNA has instructions for building and running your body.</s>
easy_definition: The molecule inside cells that contains the genetic information needed for a person and most other organisms to develop and grow and is passed from one generation to the next. Deoxyribonucleic acid is made up of two strands that twist into the shape of a spiral ladder called a double helix. Each strand has a backbone that is made up of sugar and phosphate molecules that attach to one of four bases: adenine (A), thymine (T), guanine (G), and cytosine (C). The bases pair up with one another (A with T, and G with C) to form chemical bonds, which act like rungs on a ladder. This holds the two strands of deoxyribonucleic acid together. Also called DNA.

formal_definition: mitotic rate is A measure of how fast cancer cells are dividing and growing. To find the mitotic rate, the number of cells dividing in a certain amount of cancer tissue is counted. Mitotic rate is used to help find the stage of melanoma (a type of skin cancer) and other types of cancer. Higher mitotic rates are linked with lower survival rates. Also called MR.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of "mitotic rate" in simple language for a non-medical person:

Mitotic rate is like a speedometer for cancer cells. It measures how fast those cells are dividing and growing. Imagine you have a bunch of cars on a race track, each one representing a cancer cell. If they're all moving really fast, that means the cancer cells are dividing quickly. The faster they divide, the more likely they are to spread and grow into a bigger problem. Doctors use this information to figure out how far along the cancer has progressed and what treatments might work best. Higher mitotic rates are like a red light on a dashboard, signaling that the cancer is growing faster and could be more difficult to treat.</s>
easy_definition: A measure of how fast cancer cells are dividing and growing. To find the mitotic rate, the number of cells dividing in a certain amount of cancer tissue is counted. Mitotic rate is used to help find the stage of melanoma (a type of skin cancer) and other types of cancer. Higher mitotic rates are linked with lower survival rates. Also called MR.

formal_definition: digital rectal examination is An examination in which a doctor inserts a lubricated, gloved finger into the rectum to feel for abnormalities. Also called DRE.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of a digital rectal examination (DRE) that's easy to understand and free of technical jargon:

A digital rectal examination is when a doctor gently inserts a finger into your rectum to check for any problems inside. It's like checking if there are any balls inside a ball pit - the doctor wants to make sure everything is in its place. The finger is coated with a special gel to help it slide in smoothly, just like how you might use a slimy substance to make a slippery slope more fun. During the exam, the doctor will feel around for anything unusual, like a ball that's not in its right place or something else that doesn't feel right. It's a quick and gentle procedure that can help doctors figure out if there's anything wrong inside your tummy.</s>
easy_definition: An examination in which a doctor inserts a lubricated, gloved finger into the rectum to feel for abnormalities. Also called DRE.

formal_definition: donor is In medicine, a person who gives blood, cells, tissue, or an organ for use in another person, such as in a blood transfusion or an organ transplant.
simplified_explanation:
Donor: A Special Kind of Giver

Hey there! Let's talk about something important in medicine: donors. A donor is someone who gives something valuable to help someone else who needs it. In medicine, donors give blood, cells, tissue, or organs to people who need them because their own body can't fix what's wrong. It's like when you share your favorite toy with a friend who really wants it – you're helping them out because they really need it. But instead of a toy, donors give things that can save lives or improve health. So, when someone needs a blood transfusion or an organ transplant, they might get it from a donor who gave it willingly. Pretty cool, right?</s>
easy_definition: In medicine, a person who gives blood, cells, tissue, or an organ for use in another person, such as in a blood transfusion or an organ transplant.

formal_definition: ductal carcinoma is The most common type of breast cancer. It begins in the lining of the milk ducts (thin tubes that carry milk from the lobules of the breast to the nipple). Ductal carcinoma may be either ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) or invasive ductal carcinoma. DCIS is a noninvasive condition in which abnormal cells are found in the lining of a breast duct and have not spread outside the duct to other tissues in the breast. In some cases, DCIS may become invasive cancer. In invasive ductal carcinoma, cancer has spread outside the breast duct to surrounding normal tissue. It can also spread through the blood and lymph systems to other parts of the body.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's an explanation of "ductal carcinoma" for a non-medical person:

Ductal carcinoma is a type of cancer that starts in the tiny tubes called ducts inside your breasts. These tubes carry milk from the lobules (where milk is made) to the nipple. If some of these tubes get damaged or mutate, they can turn into cancer cells. This can happen in two ways: either the cancer cells stay inside the duct and don't grow beyond it (called ductal carcinoma in situ), or they break out of the duct and go to other parts of the breast or even the rest of the body (called invasive ductal carcinoma). Either way, it's important to catch it early so we can treat it before it grows too much and becomes harder to treat.

For example, imagine a small pipe in your house that carries water from the sink to the bathroom. If some of the pipes in your house start leaking or getting clogged, you might need to fix them. Similarly, if some of the tiny tubes in your breasts start acting up, we need to check them out to make sure they don't turn into something more serious.</s>

formal_definition: NCI clinical trials cooperative group is A group of researchers, cancer centers, and community doctors who are involved in studies that test new ways to screen, prevent, diagnose, and treat cancer. Clinical trials run by cooperative groups are funded and supported by the National Cancer Institute (NCI), and large numbers of patients take part in many locations. The current U.S. cooperative groups are the Alliance for Clinical Trials in Oncology, ECOG-ACRIN Cancer Research Group, NRG Oncology, SWOG, and Children’s Oncology Group (COG). The NCI clinical trials cooperative groups are part of the National Clinical Trials Network (NCTN).

formal_definition: ejaculation is The release of semen through the penis during orgasm.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "ejaculation" to a non-medical person:

Ejaculation is when a man's body releases semen through his penis during sexual arousal or orgasm. It's like when you sneeze out a big breath - it's a natural thing that happens when your body is ready for reproduction or pleasure. When a man ejaculates, he might feel a lot of pleasure or satisfaction, and it can be a fun and normal part of being human. Just like how you might cough up mucus when you have a cold, a man's body will release semen during ejaculation. And just like how you don't want to get mucus all over the place, it's important to practice safe sex to prevent unwanted pregnancy or STDs during sexual activity.</s>

formal_definition: embolization is A procedure that uses particles, such as tiny gelatin sponges or beads, to block a blood vessel. Embolization may be used to stop bleeding or to block the flow of blood to a tumor or abnormal area of tissue. It may be used to treat some types of liver cancer, kidney cancer, and neuroendocrine tumors. It may also be used to treat uterine fibroids, aneurysms, and other conditions. Types of embolization are arterial embolization, chemoembolization, and radioembolization.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain "embolization" to a non-medical person:

Embolization is like a traffic cop for your body's blood vessels. Imagine there's a big truck driving down a busy road, and it's causing traffic jams everywhere. To fix this problem, doctors use small objects like rubber balls or jelly cubes to block the truck's path and create a detour for the blood. This helps keep the blood flowing smoothly and prevents problems like bleeding or tumors from growing bigger. There are different kinds of embolization procedures, like arterial embolization, chemoembolization, and radioembolization, each with its own special way of blocking the blood flow.

For example, imagine you have a pesky weed growing in your garden that needs to be stopped from spreading. Doctors could use embolization to block the weed's roots, so it can't grow anymore. Or, if you had a tumor in your liver, embolization could be used to block the blood vessels feeding the tumor, starving it until it shrinks away. The goal is always to improve blood flow and stop any harmful things from growing or spreading inside the body.</s>

formal_definition: endocrine therapy is Treatment that adds, blocks, or removes hormones. For certain conditions (such as diabetes or menopause), hormones are given to adjust low hormone levels. Hormones can also cause certain cancers (such as prostate and breast cancer) to grow. To slow or stop the growth of cancer, synthetic hormones or other drugs may be given to block the body’s natural hormones, or surgery is used to remove the gland that makes a certain hormone. Also called hormonal therapy, hormone therapy, and hormone treatment.
simplified_explanation: Endocrine therapy is like a delicate balance of hormones in your body. Imagine your body's hormones as a scale with different weights on each side. When you have too much or too little of a certain hormone, it can throw off the balance and cause problems. Endocrine therapy helps adjust the hormone levels back to a healthy range by adding, removing, or blocking hormones. It's like using a seesaw to balance the scales again. For example, if you have too much estrogen, endocrine therapy might add some progesterone to balance it out. Or, if you have too little insulin, endocrine therapy might give you some extra insulin to help control your blood sugar levels. The goal is to get your hormone levels just right so your body can work properly.</s>
easy_definition: Treatment that adds, blocks, or removes hormones. For certain conditions (such as diabetes or menopause), hormones are given to adjust low hormone levels. formal_definition: endoscopic ultrasound is A procedure in which an endoscope is inserted into the body. An endoscope is a thin, tube-like instrument that has a light and a lens for viewing. A probe at the end of the endoscope is used to bounce high-energy sound waves (ultrasound) off internal organs to make a picture (sonogram). Also called endosonography and EUS.
simplified_explanation: Endoscopic ultrasound is like a magic wand that helps doctors see inside your body without making big cuts or incisions. It's like a thin, long tube with a light and camera on one end, and a special device that sends sound waves through the other end. These sound waves bounce off your organs and create pictures that doctors can use to check if everything is working properly. It's kind of like when you use a flashlight to look for hidden objects in the dark, but instead of using light, the endoscope uses sound waves to find things inside you. Doctors can even use it to take tiny samples of tissue for testing, just like how you might pick up a small stone from the beach with a net. It's a pretty cool tool that helps doctors figure out what's going on inside your body!</s>

A probe at the end of the endoscope is used to bounce high-energy sound waves (ultrasound) off internal organs to make a picture (sonogram). Also called endosonography and EUS.

formal_definition: enlarged prostate is A benign (not cancer) condition in which the prostate gland is larger than normal. As the prostate gets bigger, it may press against the bladder and urethra, which can slow or block the flow of urine from the bladder. This can cause urinary symptoms, such as feeling a need to urinate often or right away, waking up many times during the night to urinate, trouble urinating, leaking urine, pain during urination or after ejaculation (the release of semen through the penis during orgasm), and blood in the urine. An enlarged prostate does not increase a person’s risk of developing prostate cancer. However, if not treated, it can lead to a weak bladder, urinary retention, urinary tract infections, bladder stones, or bladder or kidney damage. An enlarged prostate is most common in older men. Also called benign prostatic hyperplasia, benign prostatic hypertrophy, and BPH.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt at explaining "enlarged prostate" in simple language for a non-medical person:

Imagine your prostate gland is like a balloon that's inflated beyond its usual size. When it's bigger than it should be, it can put pressure on your bladder and urethra, causing problems with peeing. You might feel like you have to go more often, or it could be hard to start peeing or stop once you begin. It's not cancer, but if left untreated, it can lead to weaker bladders, infections, or even damage to other parts of your urinary system. So, it's important to see a doctor if you notice any of these symptoms.

For example, imagine you have a water balloon that's normally small and easy to handle. But one day, it starts to fill up with air and gets bigger and bigger. Eventually, it becomes too big to hold comfortably, and you might spill some water when you try to carry it. That's kind of like what happens with an enlarged prostate - it grows beyond its normal size and causes problems.</s>

formal_definition: erectile dysfunction is An inability to have an erection of the penis adequate for sexual intercourse. Also called impotence.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of erectile dysfunction (ED) in simple language:

Erectile dysfunction is when a guy has trouble getting or keeping an erection that's hard enough for sex. It's like trying to inflate a balloon, but the air won't stick inside. It's not because he doesn't want to, it's just that his body can't do it. Some guys might feel embarrassed or frustrated about it, but it's a common problem that can happen to anyone. There are ways to treat ED, like medication or talking to a doctor, so don't worry if it happens to you - there's help available.</s>
easy_definition: An inability to have an erection of the penis adequate for sexual intercourse. Also called impotence.

formal_definition: estrogen is A type of hormone made by the body that helps develop and maintain female sex characteristics and the growth of long bones. Estrogens can also be made in the laboratory. They may be used as a type of birth control and to treat symptoms of menopause, menstrual disorders, osteoporosis, and other conditions.

But did you know that there are different kinds of estrogen? Some of them are made right in your own body, and some can be made in a lab. formal_definition: surgical excision is The removal of tissue from the body using a scalpel (a sharp knife), laser, or other cutting tool. A surgical excision is usually done to remove a lump or other suspicious growth. Some normal tissue around the lump is usually removed at the same time.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining the medical term "surgical excision" in a way that's easy to understand for a non-medical person:

A surgical excision is like cutting out a piece of bad fruit from an apple. They'll take out some of the surrounding tissue too, just like how you might cut off a little bit of the rotten part to make sure you get all of it. This helps keep the rest of your body healthy and free from problems.

I hope that helps! Let me know if you have any questions.</s>

formal_definition: external beam radiation therapy is A type of radiation therapy that uses a machine to aim high-energy rays at the cancer from outside of the body. Also called external radiation therapy.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of external beam radiation therapy that's easy to understand and free of technical jargon:

External beam radiation therapy is like a superhero for your body. It's a special kind of treatment that uses a machine to send high-energy rays from outside your body to fight cancer. The rays go straight to the cancer cells and try to destroy them, just like how Superman might use his X-ray vision to stop a bad guy. But instead of using laser beams or other cool gadgets, this treatment uses big machines that are especially good at sending out really strong rays. formal_definition: simple hysterectomy is Surgery to remove the uterus and the cervix. The ovaries and fallopian tubes may also be removed. A simple hysterectomy may be done through the vagina (with no incisions in the abdomen) or through an incision (cut) in the abdomen. Also called complete hysterectomy and total hysterectomy.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of a simple hysterectomy:

A simple hysterectomy is a surgery where doctors remove a woman's uterus and cervix. It's like taking out a kitchen appliance that isn't working properly. Just like how you might throw away a broken blender, doctors can take out the uterus and cervix if they're causing problems like heavy bleeding or pain. They can do this through a small cut in the belly button or through the vagina itself. It's a common surgery that can help women feel better and live healthier lives.</s>

formal_definition: familial adenomatous polyposis is An inherited disorder in which many polyps (usually hundreds to thousands) form on the inner walls of the colon and rectum. Polyps are abnormal growths that may become cancer if they are not removed. People with familial adenomatous polyposis have a very high risk of developing colorectal cancer at an early age and are also at risk of developing cancers of the stomach, small intestine, pancreas, bile duct, liver, adrenal gland, thyroid, and brain. They may also have dental and eye problems and benign (not cancer) tumors in the stomach, small intestine, bone, soft tissue, skin, and adrenal glands. Familial adenomatous polyposis is caused by mutations (changes) in a gene called APC that are inherited in an autosomal dominant manner. It is a type of hereditary cancer syndrome. Also called classic familial adenomatous polyposis, classic familial polyposis, classic FAP, and FAP.
simplified_explanation:
Okay, let's break it down! Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) is like a garden full of weeds. Imagine your insides are a beautiful garden, and sometimes bad seeds (polyps) start growing where they shouldn't. These polyps can turn into cancer if left unchecked, so it's important to remove them before they get too big. Some people are born with a special "seed" inside them that makes it easy for these bad seeds to grow, and that's what FAP is. It's like having a special gardening problem that needs extra care.

In simpler terms, FAP is a condition where many polyps grow in the digestive system, especially in the colon and rectum. These polyps can become cancerous if they're not removed, so doctors will often recommend regular check-ups and surgeries to prevent cancer from forming. It's kind of like having a ticking time bomb inside you, and you need to take steps to defuse it before it causes any damage.</s>

formal_definition: 18F-fludeoxyglucose is A radioactive form of glucose used in positron emission tomography (PET) imaging. 18F-fludeoxyglucose gets taken up by cells in the body, and a PET scanner is used to find which cells in the body have taken it up. Since more 18F-fludeoxyglucose is taken up by abnormal cells, including cancer cells, than by normal cells, it can be used to find cancer cells in the body and to diagnose cancer and other conditions. Also called fluorine F 18-fludeoxyglucose.

formal_definition: fine-needle aspiration biopsy is The removal of fluid, cells, or tissue with a thin needle for examination under a microscope. Also called FNA biopsy.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain "fine-needle aspiration biopsy" in simple language:

A fine-needle aspiration biopsy is like taking a small sample of something for a closer look. Imagine you have a piece of food that looks suspicious, so you use a small straw to suck out just a tiny bit of it, then look at it under a magnifying glass. That's kind of like what a doctor does when they do a fine-needle aspiration biopsy. They use a very thin needle to take a small sample of fluid or tissue from somewhere in your body, like a lump or a cyst, and then they look at it under a microscope to see if there's anything unusual about it. It's like taking a tiny peek inside without having to cut open your whole body!</s>
easy_definition: The removal of fluid, cells, or tissue with a thin needle for examination under a microscope. Also called FNA biopsy.

formal_definition: first-line therapy is The first treatment given for a disease. It is often part of a standard set of treatments, such as surgery followed by chemotherapy and radiation. When used by itself, first-line therapy is the one accepted as the best treatment. If it doesn’t cure the disease or it causes severe side effects, other treatment may be added or used instead. Also called induction therapy, primary therapy, and primary treatment.
simplified_explanation:
Okay, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of "first-line therapy" in simple language that's easy to understand:

Think of first-line therapy like the starter kit for your health. It's the very first thing doctors try to help treat a sickness or disease. Sometimes it works really well and gets rid of all the problems, but if it doesn't, doctors might need to try something else. It's like trying different ingredients in a recipe until you find the right combination that tastes just right. In medicine, we call the first thing we try "first-line therapy."</s>
easy_definition: The first treatment given for a disease. It is often part of a standard set of treatments, such as surgery followed by chemotherapy and radiation. Also called induction therapy, primary therapy, and primary treatment.

formal_definition: flow cytometry is A laboratory method that measures the number of cells, the percentage of live cells, and certain characteristics of cells, such as size and shape, in a sample of blood, bone marrow, or other tissue. The presence of tumor markers, such as antigens, on the surface of the cells are also measured. The cells are stained with a light-sensitive dye, placed in a fluid, and then passed one at a time through a beam of light. The measurements are based on how the stained cells react to the beam of light. Flow cytometry is used in basic research and to help diagnose and manage certain diseases, including cancer.
simplified_explanation: Flow cytometry is like a superhero for cells! It's a way to measure important things about them, like how many there are, how many are alive, and what they look like. Imagine holding up each cell individually under a special light, like a microscope, and seeing how it reacts. This helps doctors figure out if something might be wrong with the cells, like if they're sick or have a disease. Just like how superheroes use their powers to keep us safe, flow cytometry helps keep our cells healthy!</s>
easy_definition: A laboratory method that measures the number of cells, the percentage of live cells, and certain characteristics of cells, such as size and shape, in a sample of blood, bone marrow, or other tissue. formal_definition: fluorescence in situ hybridization is A laboratory method used to look at genes or chromosomes in cells and tissues. Pieces of DNA that contain a fluorescent dye are made in the laboratory and added to a cell or tissue sample. When these pieces of DNA bind to certain genes or areas on chromosomes in the sample, they light up when viewed under a microscope with a special light. FISH can be used to identify where a specific gene is located on a chromosome, how many copies of the gene are present, and any chromosomal abnormalities. It is used to help diagnose diseases, such as cancer, and help plan treatment. Also called FISH.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's an explanation of "fluorescence in situ hybridization" that's easy to understand for a non-medical person:

Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) is like a special tool doctors use to find specific pieces of DNA inside cells and tissues. Imagine you have a flashlight and you shine it on different parts of a dark room. If there's something brightly colored in that part, you can see it easily. In the same way, FISH helps doctors see specific pieces of DNA in cells and tissues by adding a special dye that glows when it finds those pieces. This allows them to figure out where those pieces are located and if there are too many or too few of them. It's like using a flashlight to find hidden objects in a dark room!</s>

formal_definition: gastrointestinal tract is The organs that food and liquids travel through when they are swallowed, digested, absorbed, and leave the body as feces. These organs include the mouth, pharynx (throat), esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, rectum, and anus. The gastrointestinal tract is part of the digestive system. Also called alimentary tract and digestive tract.

It helps break down what you eat into tiny pieces so your body can use the nutrients it needs, and then sends the rest out as waste. Think of it like a long, winding road for your food - it goes in one end, gets broken down, and comes out the other end as poop! Pretty cool, right?</s>

formal_definition: hematologist is A doctor who has special training in diagnosing and treating blood disorders.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "hematologist" to a non-medical person:

A hematologist is a kind of doctor who specializes in taking care of people who have problems with their blood. Imagine your blood as a river that flows through your body. Just like how a river needs to be kept clean and flowing smoothly, your blood needs to be healthy too. A hematologist helps keep your blood healthy by diagnosing and treating any problems that might be causing it to become unhealthy or abnormal. They can help with things like anemia, blood clots, and even some kinds of cancer. So, if you ever have any issues with your blood, you can see a hematologist for help.</s>

formal_definition: stem cell transplant is A procedure in which a patient receives healthy stem cells (blood-forming cells) to replace their own stem cells that have been destroyed by treatment with radiation or high doses of chemotherapy. The healthy stem cells may come from the blood or bone marrow of the patient or from a related or unrelated donor. A stem cell transplant may be autologous (using a patient’s own stem cells that were collected and saved before treatment), allogeneic (using stem cells from a related or unrelated donor), syngeneic (using stem cells donated by an identical twin), or cord blood (using umbilical cord blood donated after a baby is born).
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain the term "stem cell transplant" in simple language for a non-medical person:

A stem cell transplant is like a repair crew for your body. When your body has been hurt by radiation or strong medicines, sometimes it needs help fixing itself. So, doctors take healthy stem cells from either the patient themselves or a donor and give them to the patient. These new stem cells can then help fix any damaged cells and help the body heal. There are different types of stem cell transplants, including ones using the patient's own stem cells, a related donor's stem cells, or umbilical cord blood from a newborn baby. It's like having a team of special workers come in to fix up your body!</s>
