formal_definition: human leukocyte antigen is A type of molecule found on the surface of most cells in the body. Human leukocyte antigens play an important part in the body's immune response to foreign substances. They make up a person’s tissue type, which varies from person to person. Human leukocyte antigen tests are done before a donor stem cell or organ transplant, to find out if tissues match between the donor and the person receiving the transplant. Also called HLA and human lymphocyte antigen.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my attempt at explaining human leukocyte antigen (HLA) in simple language:

Human leukocyte antigen is like a special set of markers on your cells that tell your body how to react to things that aren't supposed to be there. It's like a unique ID card for each person, and it helps your immune system figure out what belongs in your body and what doesn't. When someone gets a transplant, doctors check to see if the donated cells have the same markers as the person getting the transplant. If they do, everything will work better and there will be less chance of rejection. Think of it like a secret code between your cells and the donated cells - they need to speak the same language to work together smoothly!</s>
easy_definition: A type of molecule found on the surface of most cells in the body. Human leukocyte antigens play an important part in the body's immune response to foreign substances. They make up a person’s tissue type, which varies from person to person. Human leukocyte antigen tests are done before a donor stem cell or organ transplant, to find out if tissues match between the donor and the person receiving the transplant. Also called HLA and human lymphocyte antigen.

formal_definition: human papillomavirus is A group of more than 200 common viruses. Some human papillomavirus types, called cutaneous human papillomaviruses, are transmitted by casual contact and can infect the skin and cause common skin warts. Other human papillomavirus types, known as mucosal human papillomaviruses, can infect the moist surfaces or inner lining of some organs and body cavities, such as the cervix, vagina, vulva, penis, anus, mouth, and throat. Infection with some mucosal human papillomavirus types (collectively known as low-risk human papillomavirus) can cause warts in or around the genitals, anus, mouth, and respiratory tract but rarely cause cancer. Long-lasting infection with other mucosal human papillomavirus types (collectively known as high-risk human papillomavirus) can cause cell changes that become cancer over time if not found and removed. Human papillomavirus-related cancers include cancers of the cervix, anus, oropharynx, vagina, vulva, and penis. Mucosal human papillomavirus types are transmitted through intimate skin-to-skin contact, most often during vaginal, anal, or oral sex. Also called HPV.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of "human papillomavirus" for a non-medical person:

Human papillomavirus is a group of viruses that can affect different parts of your body, like your skin, mouth, throat, and private areas. Some types of HPV can cause warts on your skin, while others can increase your risk of getting cancer if they're inside your body for a long time. For example, HPV can cause cervix cancer in women, anal cancer in men and women, and oropharynx cancer in both men and women. It's important to know about HPV so you can take steps to protect yourself from getting infected or find it early if you do get infected. This includes getting vaccinated against HPV, practicing safe sex, and getting regular check-ups with your doctor.

I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any questions.</s>
easy_definition: A group of more than 200 common viruses. Some human papillomavirus types, called cutaneous human papillomaviruses, are transmitted by casual contact and can infect the skin and cause common skin warts. Other human papillomavirus types, known as mucosal human papillomaviruses, can infect the moist surfaces or inner lining of some organs and body cavities, such as the cervix, vagina, vulva, penis, anus, mouth, and throat. Infection with some mucosal human papillomavirus types (collectively known as low-risk human papillomavirus) can cause warts in or around the genitals, anus, mouth, and respiratory tract but rarely cause cancer. Long-lasting infection with other mucosal human papillomavirus types (collectively known as high-risk human papillomavirus) can cause cell changes that become cancer over time if not found and removed. Human papillomavirus-related cancers include cancers of the cervix, anus, oropharynx, vagina, vulva, and penis. Mucosal human papillomavirus types are transmitted through intimate skin-to-skin contact, most often during vaginal, anal, or oral sex. Also called HPV.

formal_definition: immunohistochemistry is A laboratory method that uses antibodies to check for certain antigens (markers) in a sample of tissue. The antibodies are usually linked to an enzyme or a fluorescent dye. After the antibodies bind to the antigen in the tissue sample, the enzyme or dye is activated, and the antigen can then be seen under a microscope. Immunohistochemistry is used to help diagnose diseases, such as cancer. It may also be used to help tell the difference between different types of cancer.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to explain immunohistochemistry in simple language! Here's my attempt:

Immunohistochemistry is like a treasure hunt for doctors. They use special tools to look for tiny clues called "antigens" inside a patient's tissue samples. These clues can help them figure out what's making someone sick. Think of it like searching for hidden treasures in a big box full of puzzle pieces. The doctors use special antibodies, which are like magic finder tools, to zero in on the specific treasures they want to find. When they find one, they can see it glowing brightly under a microscope, just like finding a shiny treasure in the dark! This technique helps doctors diagnose and understand diseases like cancer, and even identify which type of cancer it might be. It's like solving a mystery by following clues, one piece at a time!</s>
easy_definition: A laboratory method that uses antibodies to check for certain antigens (markers) in a sample of tissue. The antibodies are usually linked to an enzyme or a fluorescent dye. After the antibodies bind to the antigen in the tissue sample, the enzyme or dye is activated, and the antigen can then be seen under a microscope. Immunohistochemistry is used to help diagnose diseases, such as cancer. It may also be used to help tell the difference between different types of cancer.

formal_definition: in vitro fertilization is A procedure in which eggs are removed from a woman’s ovary and combined with sperm outside the body to form embryos. The embryos are grown in the laboratory for several days and then either placed in a woman’s uterus or cryopreserved (frozen) for future use. Also called IVF.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt at explaining in vitro fertilization (IVF) in simple language that's easy to understand:

IVF is like a magic trick where we help your body create a baby outside of your body. We take some of your eggs out of your tummy and mix them with some special daddy sperm in a lab. Then we wait a few days for the baby to grow and become strong enough to be planted back inside your tummy, just like how you might plant a seedling in the ground to grow into a tree. If you don't need the baby right away, we can even put it in the freezer so you can use it later. It's like putting a tiny little baby on pause!</s>
easy_definition: A procedure in which eggs are removed from a woman’s ovary and combined with sperm outside the body to form embryos. The embryos are grown in the laboratory for several days and then either placed in a woman’s uterus or cryopreserved (frozen) for future use. Also called IVF.

formal_definition: induction therapy is The first treatment given for a disease. It is often part of a standard set of treatments, such as surgery followed by chemotherapy and radiation. When used by itself, induction therapy is the one accepted as the best treatment. If it doesn’t cure the disease or it causes severe side effects, other treatment may be added or used instead. Also called first-line therapy, primary therapy, and primary treatment.
simplified_explanation: Induction therapy is like the starter kit for your body's health. It's the first treatment doctors use to try to get rid of a sickness. Sometimes it works on its own, but if it doesn't, they might add more treatments like medicine or surgery. It's like trying different ingredients in a recipe until you find the right mix to fix the problem. Doctors choose induction therapy as the first step because it's usually the most effective way to make you feel better. But if it doesn't work or causes too many side effects, they might switch to something else.</s>
easy_definition: The first treatment given for a disease. It is often part of a standard set of treatments, such as surgery followed by chemotherapy and radiation. When used by itself, induction therapy is the one accepted as the best treatment. If it doesn’t cure the disease or it causes severe side effects, other treatment may be added or used instead. Also called first-line therapy, primary therapy, and primary treatment.

formal_definition: germline mutation is A gene change in a body's reproductive cell (egg or sperm) that becomes incorporated into the DNA of every cell in the body of the offspring. Germline mutations are passed on from parents to offspring. Also called germline variant.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining "germline mutation" in simple language for a non-medical person:

A germline mutation is a change in one of your body's reproductive cells (like eggs or sperm). This change becomes a part of every cell in your body, including the cells that make you who you are. So, when you have babies, they might inherit this change from you. It's like if you had a magic pen that changed the words in a book - the changes would be there forever, even after you pass the book on to someone else. In this way, germline mutations are different from other types of mutations that happen in cells throughout your body, because they get passed on to your children.</s>
easy_definition: A gene change in a body's reproductive cell (egg or sperm) that becomes incorporated into the DNA of every cell in the body of the offspring. Germline mutations are passed on from parents to offspring. Also called germline variant.

formal_definition: intensity-modulated radiation therapy is A type of 3-dimensional radiation therapy that uses computer-generated images to show the size and shape of the tumor. Thin beams of radiation of different intensities are aimed at the tumor from many angles. This type of radiation therapy reduces the damage to healthy tissue near the tumor. Also called IMRT.
simplified_explanation: Intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) is like a personalized target practice for cancer cells. Imagine you're playing darts with your friend, and each dart represents a tiny beam of radiation. With IMRT, the radiation machine can adjust the intensity and angle of each dart to hit only the cancer cells, while avoiding the surrounding healthy tissue. It's like hitting a bullseye on a moving target! The machine uses special computers to create detailed pictures of the tumor and plan out the exact paths of the radiation beams. This way, doctors can give the cancer cells a precise dose of radiation while minimizing harm to nearby tissues. Just like how you need to aim carefully when shooting hoops, doctors need to use advanced technology to hit the cancer cells precisely with IMRT.</s>
easy_definition: A type of 3-dimensional radiation therapy that uses computer-generated images to show the size and shape of the tumor. Thin beams of radiation of different intensities are aimed at the tumor from many angles. This type of radiation therapy reduces the damage to healthy tissue near the tumor. Also called IMRT.

formal_definition: in-transit metastasis is A type of metastasis in which skin cancer spreads through a lymph vessel and begins to grow more than 2 centimeters away from the primary tumor but before it reaches the nearest lymph node.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help explain "in-transit metastasis" in a way that's easy to understand!

So, imagine your body is like a big highway system with different roads leading to different parts of your body. Cancer cells are like cars on this highway, and they can travel through your body and spread to other areas. But there's something called an "in-transit metastasis" that happens when these cancer cells get stuck in one place along the way, kind of like a traffic jam on the highway. Instead of reaching their final destination, like a nearby lymph node, these cancer cells start growing and multiplying where they got stuck, creating a new tumor. It's like a tiny satellite tumor that forms near the original tumor, but before it reaches the nearest lymph node. Does that make sense?</s>
easy_definition: A type of metastasis in which skin cancer spreads through a lymph vessel and begins to grow more than 2 centimeters away from the primary tumor but before it reaches the nearest lymph node.

formal_definition: intravenous immunoglobulin is A substance made from antibodies that have been taken from the blood of many healthy donors. It is given to a patient through a needle or tube inserted into a vein. Intravenous immunoglobulins are used to treat certain types of immune disorders in which there are low amounts of antibodies in the blood. They are also used to treat many different autoimmune disorders, infections, or other conditions. They may also be used to help prevent infections in patients who have had a stem cell or organ transplant. Intravenous immunoglobulins are a type of immunotherapy. Also called IVIG.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain "intravenous immunoglobulin" in simple language for a non-medical person:

Intravenous immunoglobulin, or IVIG, is like a superhero for your body's immune system. It's made from lots of tiny proteins called antibodies that are collected from many healthy donors. These antibodies are then mixed with a special solution and given to you through a small needle or tube inserted into a vein. The antibodies help fight off bad guys in your body, like germs and diseases, and can even help prevent infections after a stem cell or organ transplant. IVIG is like a team of protective warriors working together to keep your body safe and healthy!</s>
easy_definition: A substance made from antibodies that have been taken from the blood of many healthy donors. It is given to a patient through a needle or tube inserted into a vein. Intravenous immunoglobulins are used to treat certain types of immune disorders in which there are low amounts of antibodies in the blood. They are also used to treat many different autoimmune disorders, infections, or other conditions. They may also be used to help prevent infections in patients who have had a stem cell or organ transplant. Intravenous immunoglobulins are a type of immunotherapy. Also called IVIG.

formal_definition: intravesical chemotherapy is Treatment in which anticancer drugs are put directly into the bladder through a thin, flexible tube inserted into the urethra (the tube through which urine leaves the body from the bladder). Intravesical chemotherapy may be used to treat some types of early-stage bladder cancer.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining "intravesical chemotherapy" in simple language for a non-medical person:

Intravesical chemotherapy is a type of treatment where doctors put medicine directly into your bladder through a small tube that goes through your urethra. It's like putting medicine inside a small balloon and then inflating it inside your bladder. The medicine helps kill cancer cells in your bladder that might be causing problems. This treatment is often used to treat early-stage bladder cancer, like a small patch of cancer cells that haven't grown too far into the bladder walls yet.

For example, imagine you have a small patch of rust on your car's paint that needs to be fixed. Instead of painting over the whole car, you could use a special tool to apply a small amount of medicine directly to the rusty spot. That way, only the bad spot gets treated, and the rest of the car stays nice and shiny. Similarly, intravesical chemotherapy targets only the cancer cells in your bladder, leaving the healthy cells alone.</s>
easy_definition: Treatment in which anticancer drugs are put directly into the bladder through a thin, flexible tube inserted into the urethra (the tube through which urine leaves the body from the bladder). Intravesical chemotherapy may be used to treat some types of early-stage bladder cancer.

formal_definition: invasive cancer is Cancer that has spread beyond the layer of tissue in which it developed and is growing into surrounding, healthy tissues. Also called infiltrating cancer.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of "invasive cancer" that's easy to understand:

Invasive cancer means that the cancer cells have grown beyond the layer of tissue where they started and are now invading nearby healthy tissues. It's like a weed that grows through cracks in a sidewalk, pushing its way into other areas. Just like how weeds can take over a garden, cancer cells can grow and spread to other parts of the body, causing problems. Invasive cancer can be more difficult to treat than cancer that stays in one place because it can be hard to find all of the cancer cells.

For example, imagine you have a patch of grass on your lawn that starts to grow a little bit out of control. At first, it might just be a small patch, but if left unchecked, it could start to spread to other parts of the lawn, choking out the good grass and making it harder to keep the whole lawn looking nice. That's kind of like what happens with invasive cancer - it can start small, but quickly spread to other parts of the body if not treated.</s>
easy_definition: Cancer that has spread beyond the layer of tissue in which it developed and is growing into surrounding, healthy tissues. Also called infiltrating cancer.

formal_definition: invasive breast cancer is Cancer that has spread from where it began in the breast to surrounding normal tissue. The most common type of invasive breast cancer is invasive ductal carcinoma, which begins in the lining of the milk ducts (thin tubes that carry milk from the lobules of the breast to the nipple). Another type is invasive lobular carcinoma, which begins in the lobules (milk glands) of the breast. Invasive breast cancer can spread through the blood and lymph systems to other parts of the body. Also called infiltrating breast cancer.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's how I might explain the term "invasive breast cancer" to a non-medical person:

Invasive breast cancer means that the cancer cells have grown beyond the original tumor site in the breast and are now invading nearby tissues. Imagine your favorite snack, like a delicious cookie, that you love to munch on. But one day, some of the crumbs break off and start crawling under the fridge or into the carpet. Those crumbs are like the cancer cells, and they can spread to other areas of the kitchen or even the whole house. That's kind of like what happens with invasive breast cancer - the cancer cells keep growing and moving to new places in the body. It's important to catch this type of cancer early so we can try to stop it before it gets too far.</s>
easy_definition: Cancer that has spread from where it began in the breast to surrounding normal tissue. The most common type of invasive breast cancer is invasive ductal carcinoma, which begins in the lining of the milk ducts (thin tubes that carry milk from the lobules of the breast to the nipple). Another type is invasive lobular carcinoma, which begins in the lobules (milk glands) of the breast. Invasive breast cancer can spread through the blood and lymph systems to other parts of the body. Also called infiltrating breast cancer.

formal_definition: karyotyping is A type of genetic test that analyzes the size, shape, and number of chromosomes in a sample of cells taken from blood, amniotic fluid, bone marrow, or other tissue. Changes in the number or structure of chromosomes may be a sign of a birth defect or genetic disease or condition, such as Down syndrome, Klinefelter syndrome, or Turner syndrome. Karyotyping may also be used to help diagnose certain types of cancer, such as leukemia, lymphoma, or myeloma, and/or help plan treatment.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to explain karyotyping in a way that's easy to understand!

Think of karyotyping like taking a picture of your DNA. Just like how a photograph shows you what something looks like, a karyotype shows doctors what the inside of your cells look like, including how many chromosomes they have and how they're arranged. Changes in the number or shape of these chromosomes can be a sign of a genetic problem, like a birth defect or disease. Doctors use karyotyping to figure out if there's anything wrong with your cells and to help come up with a plan to fix it.

For example, imagine you have a box of Legos. Each Lego is like a chromosome, and when you put them all together, they form a big picture of your DNA. If some of the Legos are missing or in the wrong place, it could be a sign that something's not right. Karyotyping is like taking a picture of that Lego box to see what's inside and if everything is in order.</s>
easy_definition: A type of genetic test that analyzes the size, shape, and number of chromosomes in a sample of cells taken from blood, amniotic fluid, bone marrow, or other tissue. Changes in the number or structure of chromosomes may be a sign of a birth defect or genetic disease or condition, such as Down syndrome, Klinefelter syndrome, or Turner syndrome. Karyotyping may also be used to help diagnose certain types of cancer, such as leukemia, lymphoma, or myeloma, and/or help plan treatment.

formal_definition: lactate dehydrogenase is One of a group of enzymes found in the blood and other body tissues and involved in energy production in cells. An increased amount of lactate dehydrogenase in the blood may be a sign of tissue damage and some types of cancer or other diseases. Also called lactic acid dehydrogenase and LDH.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining lactate dehydrogenase in simple language:

Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) is an important enzyme that helps our bodies turn food into energy. It's like a worker in a factory who takes apart big molecules and turns them into smaller ones that can be used to power our bodies. When there's too much LDH in our blood, it can be a sign that something is wrong, like if we have too much sugar in our blood from exercise or illness. In some cases, high levels of LDH can also be a sign of cancer or other diseases. So, doctors use LDH tests to check if everything is working properly in our bodies.</s>
easy_definition: One of a group of enzymes found in the blood and other body tissues and involved in energy production in cells. An increased amount of lactate dehydrogenase in the blood may be a sign of tissue damage and some types of cancer or other diseases. Also called lactic acid dehydrogenase and LDH.

formal_definition: large intestine is The long, tube-like organ that is connected to the small intestine at one end and the anus at the other. The large intestine has four parts: cecum, colon, rectum, and anal canal. Partly digested food moves through the cecum into the colon, where water and some nutrients and electrolytes are removed. The remaining material, solid waste called stool, moves through the colon, is stored in the rectum, and leaves the body through the anal canal and anus.
simplified_explanation:
Okay, I'm here to help! Here's how I would explain the large intestine to a non-medical person:

So, you know how we eat food and then our bodies digest it? Well, after most of the nutrients have been taken out, what's left over goes to this really long tube called the large intestine. It's like a long, hollow pipe that's connected to your small intestine at one end and your anus at the other. Inside, there are different parts that do different things. Some food might get broken down even more in the first part, called the cecum, before moving into the rest of the colon. Then, water and some important stuff like vitamins and minerals get removed, and the rest turns into something called stool. That gets stored in the next part, called the rectum, until you go to the bathroom and it comes out through your anus. It's like a little tunnel that takes care of getting rid of the leftover food so your body can focus on taking care of all the other important stuff!</s>
easy_definition: The long, tube-like organ that is connected to the small intestine at one end and the anus at the other. The large intestine has four parts: cecum, colon, rectum, and anal canal. Partly digested food moves through the cecum into the colon, where water and some nutrients and electrolytes are removed. The remaining material, solid waste called stool, moves through the colon, is stored in the rectum, and leaves the body through the anal canal and anus.

formal_definition: large cell carcinoma is Lung cancer in which the cells are large and look abnormal when viewed under a microscope.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of "large cell carcinoma" that's easy to understand and free from technical jargon:

Large cell carcinoma is a type of lung cancer where the cancer cells are big and abnormal when looked at under a microscope. Imagine your cells as Legos - they come in different shapes and sizes, and when they grow and multiply, they usually do so in a nice, organized way. But with large cell carcinoma, the cancer cells start to grow out of control and look more like a bunch of Jenga blocks stacked on top of each other. This makes them harder to treat and more likely to spread to other parts of the body. So, just like how you wouldn't want a pile of Jenga blocks falling over and causing trouble, doctors try to catch large cell carcinoma early on before it becomes too serious.</s>
easy_definition: Lung cancer in which the cells are large and look abnormal when viewed under a microscope.

formal_definition: liver function test is A blood test to measure the blood levels of certain substances released by the liver. A high or low level of certain substances can be a sign of liver disease.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of "liver function tests" that's easy to understand and free of technical jargon:

Liver function tests are like a report card for your liver. They check how well your liver is doing by measuring the levels of certain chemicals in your blood. These chemicals are made by your liver and when they're not at the right levels, it could mean there's a problem with your liver. For example, if your liver isn't working properly, it might not be able to remove toxins from your body as well as it should, or it might produce too much of a certain chemical that can cause problems. Doing these tests can help doctors figure out if something's going on with your liver and find ways to fix it.</s>
easy_definition: A blood test to measure the blood levels of certain substances released by the liver. A high or low level of certain substances can be a sign of liver disease.

formal_definition: low-dose computed tomography is A procedure that uses a computer linked to an x-ray machine that gives off a very low dose of radiation to make a series of detailed pictures of areas inside the body. The pictures are taken from different angles and are used to create 3-D views of tissues and organs. Low-dose computed tomography is recommended as a screening test for adults who have a high risk of developing lung cancer based on their age and smoking history. Also called LDCT and low-dose CT scan.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain "low-dose computed tomography" in simple language:

Low-dose computed tomography is like a special kind of X-ray that takes really clear pictures of your insides without giving you too much radiation. It's like a magic tool that helps doctors see what's going on inside your body without harming you. They use a computer and an X-ray machine to take lots of pictures from different angles, and then they put them together to make a 3-D picture of your tissues and organs. This test is good for people who are at high risk of getting lung cancer because of their age or how much they smoke. It's like a checkup for your insides!</s>
easy_definition: A procedure that uses a computer linked to an x-ray machine that gives off a very low dose of radiation to make a series of detailed pictures of areas inside the body. The pictures are taken from different angles and are used to create 3-D views of tissues and organs. Low-dose computed tomography is recommended as a screening test for adults who have a high risk of developing lung cancer based on their age and smoking history. Also called LDCT and low-dose CT scan.

formal_definition: lumpectomy is Surgery to remove cancer or other abnormal tissue from the breast and some normal tissue around it, but not the breast itself. Some lymph nodes under the arm may be removed for biopsy. Part of the chest wall lining may also be removed if the cancer is near it. Also called breast-conserving surgery, breast-sparing surgery, partial mastectomy, quadrantectomy, and segmental mastectomy.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the term "lumpectomy" that's easy to understand:

A lumpectomy is a type of surgery where doctors remove cancer or abnormal cells from your breast, while leaving as much of your breast tissue as possible intact. It's like taking out a small bite of food from a bigger piece of cake - you want to keep as much of the good stuff as possible while getting rid of the bad part. During the surgery, doctors might also remove some lymph nodes under your armpit to check if the cancer has spread there. The goal is to remove as little tissue as possible while still getting all the bad cells.

I hope that helps! Let me know if you have any other questions.</s>
easy_definition: Surgery to remove cancer or other abnormal tissue from the breast and some normal tissue around it, but not the breast itself. Some lymph nodes under the arm may be removed for biopsy. Part of the chest wall lining may also be removed if the cancer is near it. Also called breast-conserving surgery, breast-sparing surgery, partial mastectomy, quadrantectomy, and segmental mastectomy.

formal_definition: LHRH agonist is A substance that keeps the testicles and ovaries from making sex hormones by blocking other hormones that are needed to make them. In men, LHRH agonists cause the testicles to stop making testosterone. In women, they cause the ovaries to stop making estrogen and progesterone. Some LHRH agonists are used to treat prostate cancer. Also called GnRH agonist, GnRHa, gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist, and luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone agonist.
simplified_explanation: Term 'LHRH agonist' not found in medical dictionary.
easy_definition: A substance that keeps the testicles and ovaries from making sex hormones by blocking other hormones that are needed to make them. In men, LHRH agonists cause the testicles to stop making testosterone. In women, they cause the ovaries to stop making estrogen and progesterone. Some LHRH agonists are used to treat prostate cancer. Also called GnRH agonist, GnRHa, gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist, and luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone agonist.

formal_definition: lymphatic basin is A group of lymph nodes that receives and filters lymph that flows from a certain area of the body. Special dyes may be used to stain and identify the lymphatic basin in the tissues around a tumor, so that lymph nodes that may contain cancer can be removed and checked by a pathologist.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's an explanation of the term "lymphatic basin" in simple language:

Think of your body like a network of rivers and lakes. The lymphatic basin is like a group of checkpoints along these rivers where water and waste can flow through and get filtered out. These checkpoints are called lymph nodes, and they help protect your body from germs and other bad stuff that could make you sick. When doctors need to find out if cancer cells have spread to the lymph nodes, they use special dyes to highlight the lymphatic basin in the tissues around the tumor. It's like using a flashlight to look for hidden treasure in a dark cave!</s>
easy_definition: A group of lymph nodes that receives and filters lymph that flows from a certain area of the body. Special dyes may be used to stain and identify the lymphatic basin in the tissues around a tumor, so that lymph nodes that may contain cancer can be removed and checked by a pathologist.

formal_definition: lymph vessel is A thin tube that collects lymph (clear watery fluid) from tissues in different parts of the body and returns it to the bloodstream. Lymph vessels branch, like blood vessels, throughout the body. They vary in size but are typically larger than blood capillaries and smaller than most veins. Also called lymphatic vessel.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "lymph vessel" to a non-medical person:

Lymph vessels are like tiny pipes that carry a special kind of liquid called lymph through your body. Just like how water flows through pipes in your home, lymph flows through these vessels and helps keep your body healthy by carrying away waste and extra fluids. These vessels are all over your body, even in places you can't see, like under your skin or inside your organs. They're bigger than the tiny blood vessels that carry blood, but smaller than the big veins that carry blood back to your heart. So, they play an important role in keeping your body's waste management system running smoothly!</s>
easy_definition: A thin tube that collects lymph (clear watery fluid) from tissues in different parts of the body and returns it to the bloodstream. Lymph vessels branch, like blood vessels, throughout the body. They vary in size but are typically larger than blood capillaries and smaller than most veins. Also called lymphatic vessel.

formal_definition: lymphadenectomy is A surgical procedure in which the lymph nodes are removed and a sample of tissue is checked under a microscope for signs of cancer. For a regional lymphadenectomy, some of the lymph nodes in the tumor area are removed; for a radical lymphadenectomy, most or all of the lymph nodes in the tumor area are removed. Also called lymph node dissection.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of "lymphadenectomy" that's easy to understand:

Imagine your body has a system of tiny filters called lymph nodes that help protect it from germs and sickness. Sometimes, these filters can get clogged with bad stuff, like cancer cells. That's when a doctor might do a lymphadenectomy. This is a surgery where they remove some or all of the lymph nodes that are near the tumor (the sick area) to check if there are any bad cells inside. It's kind of like taking out the trash to keep your body healthy! In a regional lymphadenectomy, only some of the lymph nodes near the tumor are taken out. In a radical lymphadenectomy, most or all of the lymph nodes in the tumor area are removed.

I hope that helps! Let me know if you have any other questions.</s>
easy_definition: A surgical procedure in which the lymph nodes are removed and a sample of tissue is checked under a microscope for signs of cancer. For a regional lymphadenectomy, some of the lymph nodes in the tumor area are removed; for a radical lymphadenectomy, most or all of the lymph nodes in the tumor area are removed. Also called lymph node dissection.

formal_definition: magnetic resonance imaging is A procedure that uses radio waves, a powerful magnet, and a computer to make a series of detailed pictures of areas inside the body. A contrast agent, such as gadolinium, may be injected into a vein to help the tissues and organs show up more clearly in the picture. Magnetic resonance imaging may be used to help diagnose disease, plan treatment, or find out how well treatment is working. It is especially useful for imaging the brain and spinal cord, the heart and blood vessels, the bones, joints, and other soft tissues, the organs in the pelvis and abdomen, and the breast. Also called MRI, NMRI, and nuclear magnetic resonance imaging.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) in simple language:

MRI is like a superpowerful camera that takes pictures of your insides without using X-rays or other harmful rays. Instead, it uses a big magnet and special waves to create detailed images of your body. The magnet is so strong that it helps your body's tissues and organs stand out from the background, kind of like how a flashlight can make things look brighter when you're playing hide-and-seek in the dark. The pictures are then sent to a computer, which can show them to doctors to help them figure out if something might be wrong with you. They can use MRI to look at almost any part of your body, like your brain, spine, heart, bones, or organs. It's like having a magic tool that can help doctors see inside your body without poking you with needles or giving you drugs!</s>
easy_definition: A procedure that uses radio waves, a powerful magnet, and a computer to make a series of detailed pictures of areas inside the body. A contrast agent, such as gadolinium, may be injected into a vein to help the tissues and organs show up more clearly in the picture. formal_definition: mammogram is An x-ray of the breast.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of the term "mammogram":

A mammogram is like a special kind of picture of your breast. It uses X-rays to take a close-up look at the tissues inside your breast, kind of like how a photographer might use a magnifying lens to get a better view of a small object. The X-rays go through your breast and create an image of the tissues on a special machine, which doctors can then look at to check for any problems or changes. Just like how you might take a picture of a bug up close to see its details, a mammogram helps doctors see the tiny details inside your breast that they couldn't otherwise see with just the naked eye.</s>
easy_definition: An x-ray of the breast.

formal_definition: mastectomy is Surgery to remove part or all of the breast. There are different types of mastectomy that differ in the amount of tissue and lymph nodes removed.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the medical term "mastectomy" in simple language that a non-medical person can understand:

A mastectomy is a surgery where a doctor removes part or all of a person's breast. There are different kinds of mastectomies, and the type of surgery depends on how much tissue and lymph nodes need to be removed. For example, a total mastectomy removes the entire breast, including the nipple and areola. A lumpectomy removes only the tumor and some surrounding tissue, leaving the rest of the breast intact. It's like taking out a bad apple from a basket - the doctor removes the part that's causing problems, while leaving the good parts untouched. The goal of the surgery is to get rid of the cancer cells and help the person feel more comfortable and confident in their own body.</s>
easy_definition: Surgery to remove part or all of the breast. There are different types of mastectomy that differ in the amount of tissue and lymph nodes removed.

formal_definition: metastasectomy is Surgery to remove one or more metastases (tumors formed from cells that have spread from the primary tumor). When all metastases are removed, it is called a complete metastasectomy.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain "metastasectomy" in simple language:

A metastasectomy is a surgery where a doctor removes one or more tumors that have grown on other parts of your body because they came from a main tumor somewhere else. If the doctor removes all the metastases during the surgery, it's called a complete metastasectomy. Imagine you have a garden with weeds growing in it. The weeds are like the metastases, and the gardener (doctor) wants to remove them so the garden (your body) can stay healthy. A complete metastasectomy is like pulling out all the weeds from the garden, leaving it looking neat and tidy again.</s>
easy_definition: Surgery to remove one or more metastases (tumors formed from cells that have spread from the primary tumor). When all metastases are removed, it is called a complete metastasectomy.

formal_definition: microsatellite is A short sequence of DNA, usually 1 to 4 basepairs (a unit of DNA), that is repeated together in a row along the DNA molecule. There is variation from person to person in the number of repeats. There are hundreds of places in human DNA that contain microsatellites.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain microsatellite in simple language for a non-medical person:

Microsatellites are like little tags on a long piece of string. They're small sequences of DNA letters (A, C, G, and T) that repeat themselves over and over again in a row. Just like how you might have a favorite color or a birthmark on your skin, each person has their own unique combination of microsatellite "tags" in their DNA. These tags can be found all over the place in your genetic material, like little markers that help scientists figure out who you are. And just like how you might have more freckles than your sibling, some people have more or fewer microsatellites than others. That's why they're so useful for studying genetics and figuring out how different traits are passed down from generation to generation!</s>
easy_definition: A short sequence of DNA, usually 1 to 4 basepairs (a unit of DNA), that is repeated together in a row along the DNA molecule. There is variation from person to person in the number of repeats. There are hundreds of places in human DNA that contain microsatellites.

formal_definition: minimal residual disease is A term used to describe a very small number of cancer cells that remain in the body during or after treatment. Minimal residual disease can be found only by highly sensitive laboratory methods that are able to find one cancer cell among one million normal cells. Checking to see if there is minimal residual disease may help plan treatment, find out how well treatment is working or if cancer has come back, or make a prognosis. Minimal residual disease testing is used mostly for blood cancers such as lymphoma and leukemia. Also called measurable residual disease and MRD.
simplified_explanation:
Okay, I'm here to help! Here's an explanation of minimal residual disease that a non-medical person could understand:

Imagine you have a big box of chocolates with lots of different types inside. The box represents your body, and each type of chocolate represents a different kind of cell. If you want to know how much chocolate is left in the box after you've eaten some, you might use a tool like a scale to measure how much is left. But what if there are just a few tiny pieces of chocolate left that are too small to measure accurately? That's kind of like what minimal residual disease means in medicine. It's when there are very few cancer cells left in your body after treatment, and doctors need special tools to find them. They can check if there are any cancer cells left by looking at a sample of your body under a microscope, kind of like counting the number of chocolate pieces left in the box. This can help doctors figure out if treatment is working, if the cancer has come back, or how likely it is that you'll get better. Doctors often use this test for blood cancers like lymphoma and leukemia.</s>
easy_definition: A term used to describe a very small number of cancer cells that remain in the body during or after treatment. Minimal residual disease can be found only by highly sensitive laboratory methods that are able to find one cancer cell among one million normal cells. Minimal residual disease testing is used mostly for blood cancers such as lymphoma and leukemia. Also called measurable residual disease and MRD.

formal_definition: mismatch repair deficiency is Describes cells that have mutations (changes) in certain genes that are involved in correcting mistakes made when DNA is copied in a cell. Mismatch repair (MMR) deficient cells usually have many DNA mutations, which may lead to cancer. MMR deficiency is most common in colorectal cancer, other types of gastrointestinal cancer, and endometrial cancer, but it may also be found in cancers of the breast, prostate, bladder, and thyroid and in an inherited disorder called Lynch syndrome. Knowing if a tumor is MMR deficient may help plan treatment or predict how well the tumor will respond to treatment. Also called deficient DNA mismatch repair, deficient mismatch repair, dMMR, and MMR deficiency.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain "mismatch repair deficiency" in simple language:

Imagine your DNA as a recipe book for your body. When your cells divide, they need to make copies of their DNA so they can grow and stay healthy. Sometimes, these copies might have mistakes in them - like a misspelling in a recipe. That's where the "mismatch repair" system comes in. It's like a team of editors who check over the copies of the DNA and fix any mistakes they find. If there are too many mistakes or if the editors aren't working properly, the mistakes can build up and cause problems. In some cases, this can lead to cancer. So, doctors might look for signs of this problem in cancer cells to see how well they might respond to treatment.

I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any questions or need further clarification.</s>
easy_definition: Describes cells that have mutations (changes) in certain genes that are involved in correcting mistakes made when DNA is copied in a cell. Mismatch repair (MMR) deficient cells usually have many DNA mutations, which may lead to cancer. MMR deficiency is most common in colorectal cancer, other types of gastrointestinal cancer, and endometrial cancer, but it may also be found in cancers of the breast, prostate, bladder, and thyroid and in an inherited disorder called Lynch syndrome. Knowing if a tumor is MMR deficient may help plan treatment or predict how well the tumor will respond to treatment. Also called deficient DNA mismatch repair, deficient mismatch repair, dMMR, and MMR deficiency.

formal_definition: monitor is In medicine, to regularly watch and check a person or condition to see if there is any change. Also refers to a device that records and/or displays patient data, such as for an electrocardiogram (EKG).
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's my explanation of the term "monitor" in medicine:

In medicine, when we say "monitor," we mean watching someone or something very closely to see if anything changes. It's like keeping an eye on your favorite sports team's score during a game - you want to know if they're winning or losing, so you keep track of the score. But instead of numbers, we're talking about people or their health conditions.

There are different kinds of monitors too. For example, a heart monitor is a machine that records and shows how your heart is beating. It's like having a personal reporter inside your body that tells you exactly what's going on with your heart at all times. And if something strange happens, like a heartbeat that's too fast or too slow, the monitor will let you and your doctor know right away.

So, whether it's a person or a machine, a monitor is like a constant observer that helps us keep track of important things happening in our bodies.</s>
easy_definition: In medicine, to regularly watch and check a person or condition to see if there is any change. Also refers to a device that records and/or displays patient data, such as for an electrocardiogram (EKG).

formal_definition: multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome is An inherited condition that may result in the development of cancers of the endocrine system. There are several types of multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome, and patients with each type may develop different types of cancer. The altered genes that cause each type can be detected with a blood test. Also called MEN syndrome.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome in simple language:

Multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome (MEN) is like a genetic puzzle that affects the parts of your body that help control growth and hormones. It's an inherited condition that can increase your risk of getting certain types of cancers, like tumors in the thyroid gland, adrenal glands, or pancreas. These tumors can grow and cause problems if they're not treated. The good news is that doctors can use a blood test to find out if you have the altered genes that cause MEN, so you can get the right treatment and stay healthy.

For example, imagine you have a set of building blocks with some missing pieces. If you have MEN, those missing pieces can cause your body to build strange structures, like tumors, instead of the normal, healthy cells it's supposed to make. It's important to find out about MEN early so you can get the right treatment and fix the problem before it gets worse.</s>
easy_definition: An inherited condition that may result in the development of cancers of the endocrine system. There are several types of multiple endocrine neoplasia syndrome, and patients with each type may develop different types of cancer. formal_definition: myelosuppression is A condition in which bone marrow activity is decreased, resulting in fewer red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets. Myelosuppression is a side effect of some cancer treatments. When myelosuppression is severe, it is called myeloablation.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's how I would explain "myelosuppression" in simple language for a non-medical person:

Myelosuppression is like a factory that produces different types of blood cells in your body. If something goes wrong with this factory, it can cause the production of red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets to decrease. This can happen as a side effect of some cancer treatments. It's like if you had a machine that made toys, but it broke down and couldn't make any more toys. Without enough red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets, your body might not be able to fight off infections or carry oxygen to different parts of your body properly. So, it's important to get medical attention if you notice any symptoms of myelosuppression.

I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any questions.</s>
easy_definition: A condition in which bone marrow activity is decreased, resulting in fewer red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets. Myelosuppression is a side effect of some cancer treatments. When myelosuppression is severe, it is called myeloablation.

formal_definition: neuroendocrine tumor is A tumor that forms from cells that release hormones into the blood in response to a signal from the nervous system. Neuroendocrine tumors may make higher-than-normal amounts of hormones, which can cause many different symptoms. These tumors may be benign (not cancer) or malignant (cancer). Some examples of neuroendocrine tumors are carcinoid tumors, islet cell tumors, medullary thyroid cancer, pheochromocytomas, neuroendocrine carcinoma of the skin (Merkel cell cancer), small cell lung cancer, and large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (a rare type of lung cancer).
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt at explaining the term "neuroendocrine tumor" in simple language:

A neuroendocrine tumor is like a messenger in your body. It's made up of cells that get signals from your nerves and then send messages to other parts of your body. These messages can affect how your body grows, works, and feels. Sometimes these cells can grow into a tumor, which is like a lump in your body. This tumor can make too much of a certain message, which can cause problems. There are different types of neuroendocrine tumors, like carcinoid tumors, islet cell tumors, and medullary thyroid cancer. Doctors will look at where the tumor is located and what kind of messages it's sending to figure out how to treat it.

I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any questions.</s>

Neuroendocrine tumors may make higher-than-normal amounts of hormones, which can cause many different symptoms. These tumors may be benign (not cancer) or malignant (cancer). Some examples of neuroendocrine tumors are carcinoid tumors, islet cell tumors, medullary thyroid cancer, pheochromocytomas, neuroendocrine carcinoma of the skin (Merkel cell cancer), small cell lung cancer, and large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (a rare type of lung cancer).

formal_definition: neuroendocrine is Having to do with the interactions between the nervous system and the endocrine system. Neuroendocrine describes certain cells that release hormones into the blood in response to stimulation of the nervous system.

Neuroendocrine refers to the way the nervous system and the hormone-producing glands in our body interact with each other. The nervous system, which includes your brain and nerves, sends signals to the hormone-producing glands, like your thyroid or adrenal glands, to release hormones into your bloodstream. These hormones help regulate important bodily functions like growth, metabolism, and mood. So, when we say something is neuroendocrine, we mean that it involves the communication between the nervous system and the hormone-producing glands to keep our bodies running smoothly.</s>
easy_definition: Having to do with the interactions between the nervous system and the endocrine system. Neuroendocrine describes certain cells that release hormones into the blood in response to stimulation of the nervous system.

formal_definition: neutrophil is A type of white blood cell that is an important part of the immune system and helps the body fight infection. When microorganisms, such as bacteria or viruses, enter the body, neutrophils are one of the first immune cells to respond. They travel to the site of infection, where they destroy the microorganisms by ingesting them and releasing enzymes that kill them. Neutrophils also boost the response of other immune cells. A neutrophil is a type of granulocyte and a type of phagocyte.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt to explain the term "neutrophil" in simple language:

Neutrophils are special cells in your body that help protect you from germs and sicknesses. Imagine your body like a castle, and these cells are the knights who defend it against invading enemies. When bad guys like bacteria or viruses try to sneak into your body, neutrophils are there to chase them away. They engulf the bad guys and break them down with special weapons, like tiny acid bombs. This helps other parts of your immune system do their job better too. So, neutrophils are superheroes of the immune system!</s>
easy_definition: A type of white blood cell that is an important part of the immune system and helps the body fight infection. formal_definition: non-small cell lung cancer is A group of lung cancers named for the kinds of cells found in the cancer and how the cells look under a microscope. The three main types of non-small cell lung cancer are adenocarcinoma (most common), squamous cell carcinoma, and large cell carcinoma. Non-small cell lung cancer is the most common of the two main types of lung cancer (non-small cell lung cancer and small cell lung cancer).
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining "non-small cell lung cancer" in simple language:

Non-small cell lung cancer is a type of cancer that starts in the lungs. It's called "non-small" because it's not as small as another type of lung cancer called "small cell lung cancer." Instead, non-small cell lung cancer is made up of different types of cells that grow and spread in different ways. There are three main types of non-small cell lung cancer: adenocarcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and large cell carcinoma. These different types of cancer have different characteristics and can be treated in different ways. Non-small cell lung cancer is the most common type of lung cancer, and it's important to get it checked out by a doctor if you notice any symptoms like coughing or chest pain.</s>

The three main types of non-small cell lung cancer are adenocarcinoma (most common), squamous cell carcinoma, and large cell carcinoma. formal_definition: oocyte cryopreservation is The process of freezing one or more unfertilized eggs (eggs that have not been combined with sperm) to save them for future use. The eggs are thawed and fertilized in the laboratory to make embryos that can be placed in a woman’s uterus. Oocyte cryopreservation is being studied as a type of fertility preservation. It may be useful for women with cancer who want to have children after having radiation therapy, chemotherapy, or certain types of surgery, which can cause infertility. Also called egg banking, egg cryopreservation, and egg freezing.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt to explain oocyte cryopreservation in simple language:

Oocyte cryopreservation is like saving seeds for planting later. Instead of saving seeds from fruits and vegetables, we freeze unfertilized eggs (oocytes) from a woman's body. These frozen eggs can then be thawed and fertilized in a lab to create embryos that can be put back into the woman's uterus when she's ready to have a baby. This can be helpful for women who might lose their ability to have kids due to cancer treatment, surgery, or other reasons. It's also known as egg banking or egg freezing. Just like how you might want to save some extra money for a rainy day, these frozen eggs can provide a "rainy day" option for building a family in the future.</s>

formal_definition: orchiectomy is Surgery to remove one or both testicles. Also called orchidectomy.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the term "orchiectomy" in simple language that a non-medical person could understand:

An orchiectomy is a surgery where a doctor removes one or both testicles. It's like taking out a small ball from your body. This surgery is usually done to treat certain health problems, such as cancer, or to prevent testicular torsion, which is when the testicle twists and cuts off blood flow. The testicles are important for making sperm and hormones, so removing them can affect fertility and hormone levels. The surgery is usually done under general anesthesia, which means you won't feel anything during the procedure. Afterwards, you may need to take some time to recover and rest.</s>
easy_definition: Surgery to remove one or both testicles. Also called orchidectomy.

formal_definition: palliative therapy is Treatment given to help relieve the symptoms and reduce the suffering caused by cancer or other life-threatening diseases. Palliative therapy may help a person feel more comfortable, but it does not treat or cure the disease. For example, surgery, radiation therapy, or chemotherapy may be given to remove, shrink, or slow the growth of a tumor that is causing pain. Palliative therapy may be given with other treatments from the time of diagnosis until the end of life.
simplified_explanation:
Okay! Here's my attempt at explaining "palliative therapy" in simple language for a non-medical person:

Palliative therapy is like a special kind of medicine that doctors give to people who are very sick, like cancer. It helps make them feel better and more comfortable, even if it doesn't fix the sickness itself. It's like giving someone a warm blanket on a cold night - it might not change the weather, but it makes them feel cozy and more relaxed. Palliative therapy can help with things like pain, nausea, and breathing problems, and doctors might use it along with other treatments like surgery or chemotherapy. The goal is to make the person as comfortable as possible until they get better or until they pass away.</s>
easy_definition: Treatment given to help relieve the symptoms and reduce the suffering caused by cancer or other life-threatening diseases. Palliative therapy may help a person feel more comfortable, but it does not treat or cure the disease. For example, surgery, radiation therapy, or chemotherapy may be given to remove, shrink, or slow the growth of a tumor that is causing pain. Palliative therapy may be given with other treatments from the time of diagnosis until the end of life.

formal_definition: partial response is A decrease in the size of a tumor, or in the extent of cancer in the body, in response to treatment. Also called partial remission.
simplified_explanation:
Okay, here's my attempt at explaining the term "partial response" in a way that's easy to understand for a non-medical person:

When we talk about cancer, we often use the word "response" to describe how well treatment is working. "Partial response" means that the cancer is getting smaller, but not completely gone. It's like trying to clean up a big mess with a small broom - you might be able to clear out some of the clutter, but there's still more work to do. In the same way, partial response in cancer means that the treatment has helped reduce the size of the tumor or the spread of the cancer, but there's still more work to do to get rid of all of it. So, while it's good news that the cancer is shrinking, it's important to keep going with treatment until it's completely gone.</s>
easy_definition: A decrease in the size of a tumor, or in the extent of cancer in the body, in response to treatment. Also called partial remission.

formal_definition: pathological staging is A method used to find out the stage of cancer (amount or spread of cancer in the body) by removing tissue samples during surgery or a biopsy. The pathological stage is based on how different from normal the cells in the samples look under a microscope.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's my attempt at explaining pathological staging in simple language for a non-medical person:

Pathological staging is like a detective story for doctors. They take tiny pieces of your body, called biopsies, and look at them under a special microscope to figure out how much cancer there is and where it's hiding in your body. Just like a detective looks for clues to solve a mystery, the doctor looks for signs of cancer in the biopsy samples to determine the "stage" of the cancer. The stage tells them how far the cancer has spread, so they can give you the best treatment plan. Imagine you have a puzzle with many pieces, and each piece represents a cell in your body. If most of the pieces are normal, that means the cancer hasn't spread too far. But if some pieces are cancerous, that means the cancer has spread to those areas. Doctors use pathological staging to figure out which pieces are cancerous and where they are located, so they can fix the problem.</s>
easy_definition: A method used to find out the stage of cancer (amount or spread of cancer in the body) by removing tissue samples during surgery or a biopsy. The pathological stage is based on how different from normal the cells in the samples look under a microscope.

formal_definition: peptide receptor radionuclide therapy is A type of radiation therapy used to treat certain types of neuroendocrine tumors. A radioactive chemical is linked to a peptide (small protein) that targets cancer cells. When this radioactive peptide is injected into the body, it binds to a specific receptor found on some cancer cells. The radioactive peptide builds up in these cells and helps kill the cancer cells without harming normal cells. Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy is a type of targeted therapy. Also called PRRT.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's an explanation of peptide receptor radionuclide therapy for a non-medical person:

Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy is like a special kind of medicine that doctors use to fight cancer. It works by using a tiny protein called a peptide that can find and attach to cancer cells. This peptide is mixed with a small amount of radiation, which makes it very powerful against the cancer cells. When the peptide and radiation are inside the body, they go to the cancer cells and help kill them without hurting other healthy cells. It's like a smart bomb that only attacks the bad guys!

For example, imagine you have a group of invading army soldiers who are hiding in a forest. You could send in a team of soldiers who know how to find and defeat the enemy soldiers, but you wouldn't want to hurt any innocent civilians in the process. That's what peptide receptor radionuclide therapy does - it finds and kills the bad cancer cells while leaving the good cells alone.</s>
easy_definition: A type of radiation therapy used to treat certain types of neuroendocrine tumors. A radioactive chemical is linked to a peptide (small protein) that targets cancer cells. When this radioactive peptide is injected into the body, it binds to a specific receptor found on some cancer cells. The radioactive peptide builds up in these cells and helps kill the cancer cells without harming normal cells. Peptide receptor radionuclide therapy is a type of targeted therapy. Also called PRRT.

formal_definition: peripheral blood is Blood circulating throughout the body.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain "peripheral blood" to a non-medical person:

Peripheral blood refers to the blood that flows through your body outside of your heart and major blood vessels. It's like the water in a garden hose - it moves around and reaches all parts of your body, providing oxygen and nutrients to different cells and organs. Just as water flows through a hose, peripheral blood flows through tiny vessels called capillaries, which are too small for doctors to see with the naked eye. So, when we check your blood, we're actually checking the quality of the blood flowing through these tiny vessels.</s>
easy_definition: Blood circulating throughout the body.

formal_definition: physical examination is An exam of the body to check for general signs of disease.
simplified_explanation:

A physical examination is like a detective searching for clues about your health. A doctor will use their senses - sight, touch, hearing, and sometimes even smell or taste - to look for any signs of disease or problems inside your body. They might check your heart rate, listen to your lungs, feel your abdomen, and even shine a light into your ears and eyes. Just like a detective looks for clues to solve a mystery, a doctor uses a physical examination to find out what might be causing your symptoms or illness. It's an important part of diagnosing and treating medical problems!</s>
easy_definition: An exam of the body to check for general signs of disease.

formal_definition: platelet is A tiny, disc-shaped piece of cell that is found in the blood and spleen. Platelets are pieces of very large cells in the bone marrow called megakaryocytes. They help form blood clots to slow or stop bleeding and to help wounds heal. Having too many or too few platelets or having platelets that don’t work as they should can cause problems. Checking the number of platelets in the blood may help diagnose certain diseases or conditions. Also called thrombocyte.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of the term "platelet" in simple language:

Platelets are like tiny building blocks that are found in your blood and spleen. They're actually pieces of big cells called megakaryocytes that are made in your bone marrow. When you get hurt or cut, platelets help stop the bleeding by forming clots that seal the wound. If you have too many or too few platelets, it can cause problems like excessive bleeding or difficulty healing. Doctors can check how many platelets you have in your blood to help diagnose certain illnesses or conditions. So, platelets are like little superheroes that keep you safe and healthy!</s>

They help form blood clots to slow or stop bleeding and to help wounds heal. Having too many or too few platelets or having platelets that don’t work as they should can cause problems. Checking the number of platelets in the blood may help diagnose certain diseases or conditions. Also called thrombocyte.

formal_definition: polymerase chain reaction is A laboratory method used to make many copies of a specific piece of DNA from a sample that contains very tiny amounts of that DNA. Polymerase chain reaction allows these pieces of DNA to be amplified so they can be detected. Polymerase chain reaction may be used to look for certain changes in a gene or chromosome, which may help find and diagnose a genetic condition or a disease, such as cancer. It may also be used to look at pieces of the DNA of certain bacteria, viruses, or other microorganisms to help diagnose an infection. Also called PCR.
simplified_explanation:

Think of PCR like a copy machine for DNA. Just like how a copy machine makes multiple copies of a document, PCR makes many copies of a small piece of DNA from a sample. This allows doctors to detect even tiny amounts of a specific gene or chromosome in a patient's body, which can help diagnose diseases like cancer or genetic conditions. It's like using a magnifying glass to see something really small, but instead of looking at something through a lens, we use special chemicals and machines to make lots of copies of a tiny piece of DNA.

PCR is super helpful because it can find even the tiniest changes in a gene or chromosome, like finding a needle in a haystack. And just like how you might use a flashlight to look for a specific object in a dark room, PCR can help doctors find specific pieces of DNA in a patient's body. So, it's like having a special tool that helps doctors see what's going on at a really small scale!</s>

If a polyp grows in one of those places, it can cause problems like blockages or pain. Doctors can remove polyps if they become a problem.

Here are some everyday analogies that might help:

formal_definition: positron emission tomography scan is A procedure in which a small amount of radioactive glucose (sugar) is injected into a vein, and a scanner is used to make detailed, computerized pictures of areas inside the body where the glucose is taken up. Because cancer cells often take up more glucose than normal cells, the pictures can be used to find cancer cells in the body. Also called PET scan.

Then, they use a machine that takes pictures of your body from many angles, like a big, fancy camera. The machine can see where the sugar goes inside your body and helps doctors find any places where there might be cancer cells. formal_definition: positron emission tomography-computed tomography scan is A procedure that combines the pictures from a positron emission tomography (PET) scan and a computed tomography (CT) scan. The PET and CT scans are done at the same time with the same machine. The combined scans give more detailed pictures of areas inside the body than either scan gives by itself. A positron emission tomography-computed tomography scan may be used to help diagnose disease, such as cancer, plan treatment, or find out how well treatment is working. Also called PET-CT scan.

formal_definition: prostate-specific antigen is A protein made by the prostate gland and found in the blood. Prostate-specific antigen blood levels may be higher than normal in men who have prostate cancer, benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), or infection or inflammation of the prostate gland. Also called PSA.

When this protein gets into your bloodstream, it can give doctors clues about how your prostate gland is doing. So, doctors might check your PSA levels to help figure out what's going on and come up with a plan to fix it. That's pretty much it!</s>

formal_definition: prostate-specific membrane antigen is A protein that is usually found on the surface of normal prostate cells but is found in higher amounts on prostate cancer cells. Prostate-specific membrane antigen may be used as a target in imaging to help find prostate cancer cells, especially those that may have come back or spread to other parts of the body. Research is being done to learn more about prostate-specific membrane antigen and how it may also be used as a target for certain anticancer drugs. Also called PSMA.

formal_definition: proton beam radiation therapy is A type of radiation therapy that uses streams of protons (tiny particles with a positive charge) to kill tumor cells. This type of treatment can reduce the amount of radiation damage to healthy tissue near a tumor. It is used to treat cancers of the head and neck and organs such as the brain, eye, lung, spine, and prostate. Proton beam radiation is different from x-ray radiation.

So, it's like a super precise weapon that helps doctors fight cancer without hurting much else. They use special machines called linacs to fire these proton beams at the cancer cells, and it's usually used for cancers in the head or body. Just like how you might use a magnifying glass to focus sunlight on a pesky bug, proton beams help doctors focus their attack on the bad cells while leaving everything else intact.</s>

formal_definition: pulmonary function test is A test used to measure how well the lungs work. It measures how much air the lungs can hold and how quickly air is moved into and out of the lungs. It also measures how much oxygen is used and how much carbon dioxide is given off during breathing. A pulmonary function test can be used to diagnose a lung disease and to see how well treatment for the disease is working. Also called lung function test and PFT.

formal_definition: radical prostatectomy is Surgery to remove the entire prostate and some of the tissue around it, including the seminal vesicles (a gland that helps make semen). Nearby lymph nodes may also be removed. A radical prostatectomy may be done through an incision (cut) made in the wall of the lower abdomen or in the perineum (the area between the scrotum and anus). It may also be done using a laparoscope (a thin, tube-like instrument with a light and lens for viewing) or a special robotic system. An attempt is made to protect the nerves that control penile erection and the bladder from damage.

Red blood cells contain a protein called hemoglobin, which carries oxygen from the lungs to all parts of the body. Checking the number of red blood cells in the blood is usually part of a complete blood cell (CBC) test. It may be used to look for conditions such as anemia, dehydration, malnutrition, and leukemia. Also called erythrocyte and RBC.

formal_definition: refractory is In medicine, describes a disease or condition that does not respond to treatment.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's how I would explain the term "refractory" to a non-medical person:

Refractory means something that doesn't change or improve even after treatment. Imagine trying to fix a broken toy with glue, but no matter how hard you try, it still doesn't work. The toy is like the disease or condition, and the glue is the treatment. If the toy doesn't fix, it's refractory. In medicine, we use this term when a patient's condition doesn't get better despite our best efforts to treat it. It's like trying to put a puzzle together with missing pieces - no matter how hard we try, we can't make it fit. So, we might need to try different treatments or seek help from someone else.</s>
easy_definition: In medicine, describes a disease or condition that does not respond to treatment.

formal_definition: regimen is A treatment plan that specifies the dosage, the schedule, and the duration of treatment.
simplified_explanation:

A regimen is like a recipe for your body. It tells you exactly what to do and when to do it in order to feel better. Just like a recipe has steps for making a delicious meal, a regimen has steps for treating your illness or condition. The difference is, instead of ingredients and cooking times, a regimen gives you the right medication, the right dose, and the right schedule to take it. This helps your body get better faster and stay healthy longer. For example, if you have high blood pressure, your doctor might give you a regimen that says to take your medicine every day at breakfast and dinner, and to also exercise and eat healthy foods to help lower your blood pressure.</s>
easy_definition: A treatment plan that specifies the dosage, the schedule, and the duration of treatment.

formal_definition: clinical resistance is The failure of a cancer to shrink after treatment.
simplified_explanation:
Sure, I'd be happy to help! Here's an explanation of "clinical resistance" that a non-medical person could understand:

Clinical resistance means when cancer cells don't respond or stop growing after treatment. It's like trying to defeat an enemy in a game of tag, but they keep running away and dodging your attacks. No matter how hard you try, you just can't seem to catch them. In cancer, the cells that are supposed to die from treatment don't, so the tumor keeps growing. This makes it harder to treat and can lead to a poorer outcome.

For example, imagine you have a group of bad guys in a video game who are really good at hiding and dodging your attacks. No matter how many times you try to defeat them, they just won't go down. That's kind of like what happens with clinical resistance in cancer.</s>
easy_definition: The failure of a cancer to shrink after treatment.

formal_definition: risk factor is Something that increases the chance of developing a disease. Some examples of risk factors for cancer are age, a family history of certain cancers, use of tobacco products, being exposed to radiation or certain chemicals, infection with certain viruses or bacteria, and certain genetic changes.

formal_definition: sentinel lymph node biopsy is Removal and examination of the sentinel node(s) (the first lymph node(s) to which cancer cells are likely to spread from a primary tumor). To identify the sentinel lymph node(s), the surgeon injects a radioactive substance, blue dye, or both near the tumor. The surgeon then uses a probe to find the sentinel lymph node(s) containing the radioactive substance or looks for the lymph node(s) stained with dye. The surgeon then removes the sentinel node(s) to check for the presence of cancer cells.
