formal_definition: somatostatin receptor scintigraphy is A type of radionuclide scan used to find carcinoid and other types of tumors. Radioactive octreotide, a drug similar to somatostatin, is injected into a vein and travels through the bloodstream. The radioactive octreotide attaches to tumor cells that have receptors for somatostatin. A radiation-measuring device detects the radioactive octreotide, and makes pictures showing where the tumor cells are in the body. Also called octreotide scan and SRS.

These receptors are found on some tumor cells, and when the octreotide attaches to them, it helps the doctor see where the tumors are located using a special machine that can spot the radioactivity. It's like a treasure hunt for the doctors to find the tumors and figure out how to treat them!</s>

formal_definition: sporadic cancer is Cancer that occurs in people who do not have a family history of that cancer or an inherited change in their DNA that would increase their risk for that cancer.
simplified_explanation:
Sure! Here's how I would explain the term "sporadic cancer" to a non-medical person:

Cancer is a disease where some of your body's cells grow and multiply in a way that's not normal. When someone has sporadic cancer, it means that they don't have a family history of the cancer or any genetic changes that make them more likely to get it. It's like finding a random pebble on the beach - you didn't know it was there until you found it, and it doesn't run in your family. In contrast, if you have a family history of cancer, it's like having a map to find the pebble - you know where it's likely to be. So, sporadic cancer is when the cancer just happens randomly without any clear cause.</s>
easy_definition: Cancer that occurs in people who do not have a family history of that cancer or an inherited change in their DNA that would increase their risk for that cancer.

formal_definition: squamocolumnar junction is The area around the opening of the cervix where the endocervix (inner part) and ectocervix (outer part) come together. This area contains both glandular cells (cells that make mucus) from the endocervix and squamous cells (thin, skin-like cells) from the ectocervix. Most abnormal cell changes and most cervical cancers begin in the squamous cells of the squamocolumnar junction. Also called transformation zone.

The glandular cells are like tiny factories that produce mucus to help protect the cervix and keep it healthy. The squamous cells are like thin, flat sheets that cover the surface of the cervix. Now, where these two types of cells meet at the opening of the cervix is called the squamocolumnar junction. This area is important because it's where most abnormal cell changes and most cervical cancers start. So, when doctors check for cancer, they look closely at this area to see if anything is going on.

In simpler terms, the squamocolumnar junction is like a special place inside your cervix where two different types of cells meet. These cells play important roles in keeping your cervix healthy, and changes in this area can be a sign of something gone wrong. Doctors check this area carefully to find any problems early on, which can help prevent more serious issues from developing.</s>

formal_definition: SABR is A type of external radiation therapy that uses special equipment to position a patient and precisely deliver radiation to tumors in the body (except the brain). The total dose of radiation is divided into smaller doses given over several days. This type of radiation therapy helps spare normal tissue. Also called SBRT, stereotactic ablative body radiation therapy, and stereotactic body radiation therapy.

formal_definition: stereotactic body radiation therapy is A type of external radiation therapy that uses special equipment to position a patient and precisely deliver radiation to tumors in the body (except the brain). Also called SABR, SBRT, and stereotactic ablative body radiation therapy.

Stereotactic body radiation therapy is like a super precise and focused beam of light that doctors use to treat cancer in your body. They use special machines to position you just right and aim the light at the tumor. It's like using a laser to target a tiny spot on a distant planet - the light is so concentrated it can only hit that one spot and avoid everything else around it. Doctors give you lots of little doses of this light over several days instead of one big dose, which helps keep nearby healthy tissues safe from damage. It's called SBR, SBRT, or stereotactic ablative body radiation therapy, and it's a really cool way to fight cancer without hurting much of anything else in your body!</s>

formal_definition: stereotactic radiosurgery is A type of external radiation therapy that uses special equipment to position the patient and precisely give a single large dose of radiation to a tumor. It is used to treat brain tumors and other brain disorders that cannot be treated by regular surgery. It is also being studied in the treatment of other types of cancer. Also called radiation surgery, radiosurgery, and stereotaxic radiosurgery.

It's like a magic wand that can give your doctor a very precise and powerful dose of radiation to that tiny spot, without harming any of the nearby healthy tissue. It's like a laser beam that only hits the bad guy (the tumor) and leaves everything else alone. This is a great option for people who have brain tumors or other brain problems that can't be fixed with surgery.

But with stereotactic radiosurgery, you could use a special machine to target the mosquito with a super-powerful bug spray (radiation) that only affects the mosquito and not your hand. formal_definition: steroid is Any of a group of lipids (fats) that have a certain chemical structure. Steroids occur naturally in plants and animals or they may be made in the laboratory. Examples of steroids include sex hormones, cholesterol, bile acids, and some drugs.

formal_definition: survivorship care plan is A detailed plan given to a patient after treatment ends, that contains a summary of the patient’s treatment, along with recommendations for follow-up care. In cancer, the plan is based on the type of cancer and the treatment the patient received. A survivorship care plan may include schedules for physical exams and medical tests to see if the cancer has come back or spread to other parts of the body. Getting follow-up care also helps check for health problems that may occur months or years after treatment ends, including other types of cancer. A survivorship care plan may also include information to help meet the emotional, social, legal, and financial needs of the patient. It may include referrals to specialists and recommendations for a healthy lifestyle, such as changes in diet and exercise and quitting smoking. Also called follow-up care plan.

formal_definition: talimogene laherparepvec is A drug used to treat melanoma that has recurred (come back) after surgery. It is used in patients whose cancer is in the skin and lymph nodes and cannot be removed by surgery. It is also being studied in the treatment of other types of cancer. Talimogene laherparepvec is made with a form of the herpesvirus that has been changed in the laboratory to infect and break down cancer cells without harming normal cells. It may also help the immune system kill cancer cells. Talimogene laherparepvec is injected directly into tumors in the skin and lymph nodes. It is a type of oncolytic virus therapy. Also called Imlygic and T-VEC.

formal_definition: 3-dimensional conformal radiation therapy is A procedure that uses a computer to create a 3-dimensional picture of the tumor. This allows doctors to give the highest possible dose of radiation to the tumor, while sparing the normal tissue as much as possible. Also called 3-dimensional radiation therapy and 3D-CRT.

They then use these pictures to create a 3D picture of the tumor, like putting all the puzzle pieces together. This helps doctors give the most precise and powerful treatment to the tumor while avoiding harming nearby healthy tissues, like surrounding puzzle pieces. It's like giving the right amount of force to the right puzzle piece to make it fit perfectly without breaking any others.

They then use this picture to plan the best way to give radiation to the tumor while protecting nearby healthy tissues. easy_definition: A procedure that uses a computer to create a 3-dimensional picture of the tumor. This allows doctors to give the highest possible dose of radiation to the tumor, while sparing the normal tissue as much as possible. Also called 3-dimensional radiation therapy and 3D-CRT.

formal_definition: tocilizumab is A drug used to treat adults and children aged 2 years and older with severe or life-threatening cytokine release syndrome caused by a type of immunotherapy called chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy. Tocilizumab is also used to treat certain types of arthritis and a condition that causes inflammation of the lining of the arteries. It is also being studied in the treatment of other conditions. Tocilizumab binds to receptors for a protein called interleukin-6 (IL-6), which is made by white blood cells and other cells in the body. This may help lower the body’s immune response and reduce inflammation. Tocilizumab is a type of monoclonal antibody. Also called Actemra.

formal_definition: tumor lysis syndrome is A condition that can occur after treatment of a fast-growing cancer, especially certain leukemias and lymphomas (cancers of the blood). As tumor cells die, they break apart and release their contents into the blood. This causes a change in certain chemicals in the blood, which may cause damage to organs, including the kidneys, heart, and liver.

formal_definition: intravenous urogram is An x-ray image of the kidneys, ureters, and bladder, and prostate in men, that is taken after a contrast dye is injected into a vein. As the dye travels through the bloodstream, it collects in these organs, turning them bright white. This allows the health care provider to see blockages or other urinary tract problems on the x-ray pictures. An intravenous urogram may be used to help diagnose certain urinary tract conditions, such as kidney or bladder stones, kidney cysts, urinary tract tumors, and an enlarged prostate. Also called intravenous pyelogram, IVP, and IVU.

term: stage 0 transitional cell carcinoma in situ of the renal pelvis and ureter
formal_definition: stage 0 transitional cell carcinoma in situ of the renal pelvis and ureter is Abnormal cells are found in tissue lining the inside of the renal pelvis or ureter. Stage 0a is also called noninvasive papillary carcinoma, which may look like long, thin growths that grow out from the tissue lining the inside of the renal pelvis or ureter. Stage 0is is also called carcinoma in situ, which is a flat tumor on the tissue lining the inside of the renal pelvis or ureter.

Sometimes, these cells can get sick and turn into bad guys, called cancer cells. When this happens, they can start growing and invading the good cells around them, like a weed taking over a garden. But, sometimes the cancer cells don't grow far and stay close to where they started, like a small patch of grass that doesn't spread too far. This is called stage 0 transitional cell carcinoma in situ of the renal pelvis and ureter. It's like a tiny patch of grass that needs to be watched closely to make sure it doesn't grow and cause trouble.

So, stage 0 means that the cancer is still in its early stages and hasn't grown much beyond where it started. There are two types of stage 0: stage 0a and stage 0is. formal_definition: vacuum-assisted core biopsy is A procedure in which a small sample of tissue is removed from the breast. An imaging device is used to guide a hollow probe connected to a vacuum device. The probe is inserted through a tiny cut made in numbed skin on the breast. The tissue sample is removed using gentle vacuum suction and a small rotating knife within the probe. Then the tissue sample is studied under a microscope to check for signs of disease. This procedure causes very little scarring and no stitches are needed. Also called VACB and vacuum-assisted biopsy.

Then, they carefully insert a thin needle into your breast through a tiny cut, and use a little bit of suction to grab onto the tissue they want to take a look at. formal_definition: vasoactive intestinal peptide is A hormone found in the pancreas, intestine, and central nervous system. It has many actions in the body, such as helping to control the secretion of water, salts, enzymes, and gastric acid during digestion. It also causes smooth muscles in the digestive tract, the heart, and the blood vessels to relax. It also has effects on the immune system and the central nervous system. Certain tumors in the pancreas make large amounts of vasoactive intestinal peptide. Also called VIP hormone.

formal_definition: white blood cell is A type of blood cell that is made in the bone marrow and found in the blood and lymph tissue. White blood cells are part of the body’s immune system. They help the body fight infection and other diseases. Types of white blood cells are granulocytes (neutrophils, eosinophils, and basophils), monocytes, and lymphocytes (T cells and B cells). Checking the number of white blood cells in the blood is usually part of a complete blood cell (CBC) test. It may be used to look for conditions such as infection, inflammation, allergies, and leukemia. Also called leukocyte and WBC.
